If the behaviour is treated in isolation without taking into consideration the issues and effects of racism, this can be described as institutional racism. The racist element must be explicitly recognised and dealt with.

This type of racism is also covert and indirect and therefore can be difficult to identify and address. It is often evident in the assumptions, beliefs, and values that affect people’s instinctive responses. It can be demonstrated subconsciously in subtle ways, and Imagine Montessori School recognises the need consciously to challenge such attitudes.

**b. Personal Racism**

This is often direct and identifiable and can be manifested through harassment and offensive behaviour in the following ways:

*   Physical assault against a person or group due to their colour, ethnicity, geographical origin, or culture.
*   Derogatory name-calling, insults, ridiculing, and racist jokes.
*   Racist graffiti.
*   Provocative behaviour such as wearing racist badges or insignia.
*   Verbal abuse and threats.
*   Incitement of others to behave in a racist way.
*   Racist comments within the context of lessons.

nodes_flat.title: Accountability, Responsibility, and Action Regarding Racism
nodes_flat.summary: The Anti-Racism Policy mandates the Senior Leadership Team and Headteachers ensure staff promote racial equality, challenge racist/geographically biased attitudes, and report suspected racism to HTs. All reported racist incidents *must* be investigated and recorded using Incident Report Forms, following our Behaviour Policy. Disciplinary actions for students include Headteacher warnings, exclusion from co-curricular activities, or temporary/permanent exclusion for continued racist behaviour. Victims' satisfaction is crucial, and they have the legal right to report racial crimes to police. New staff are inducted, and the policy undergoes a formal review annually, with amendments circulated to staff and families.
nodes_flat.content:
The Senior Leadership Team is responsible for ensuring that the school meets its commitments under its Anti-Racism Policy. In addition, they will provide any necessary help to staff to keep up-to-date with any changes in race relations legislation.

*   The Headteachers will ensure that all staff are aware of their responsibilities and are given the support to exercise this responsibility.
*   All racist incidents that are reported will need to be investigated and recorded.
*   All staff are required to promote racial equality and good race relations.
*   Staff are expected not to discriminate on racial grounds and are expected to attend training or to read information provided by the Headteachers about any change to policy.
*   Staff are expected to always challenge racist and geographically biased attitudes and behaviour.
*   Staff are also expected to be aware of the need to report suspicions of racism to the Headteacher.
*   Where appropriate, the school will be prepared to contact the police if a racial crime has been committed.

Alleged incidents of racism should be investigated in line with our Behaviour Policy. We have Incident Report Forms (school drive) which can be used to help systematically record incident details as the first part of an investigation.

Possible disciplinary action by the school:

*   An official warning from the Headteacher.
*   Exclusion from certain areas of school premises and life (such as co-curricular activities).
*   In serious cases or where there is repeated racism, exclusion (temporary) from the school for a fixed term.
*   Continued racist behaviour will be incompatible with staying at the school, and permanent exclusion may be used in such cases.

It is important that the victim of the racist incident is satisfied with the outcome of any disciplinary measures taken. It is also important to remember that anyone who has been a victim of a racist incident has the legal right to report it as a crime to the police.

See the school’s Anti-Bullying Policy for further details about our response to bullying in general.

**Implementing and Reviewing the Anti-Racism Policy**

New staff will be introduced to the policy during their induction.

There will be a formal review of the policy every year, and any necessary amendments will be circulated to staff and families.

nodes_flat.title: 5.8. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The Anti-Racism Policy undergoes an annual review by the Leadership Team. This process ensures its effectiveness and compliance with current legislation and best practices. Crucially, input is actively sought from staff, students, and parents to guarantee the policy addresses community needs. All updates or changes are then promptly communicated to stakeholders.
nodes_flat.content: The Anti-Racism Policy will be reviewed annually by the Leadership Team to ensure its effectiveness and alignment with current legislation and best practices. Input from staff, students, and parents will be sought to ensure the policy reflects the needs of the community. Any updates or changes will be communicated promptly to all stakeholders.

nodes_flat.title: 5.9. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: Key reference documents include the Behaviour Policy, detailing school rules and student conduct. The Anti-Bullying Policy outlines how bullying is addressed. Incident Report Forms provide a process for logging concerns. Also referenced is relevant legislation, specifically the Equality Act 2010, ensuring fair treatment and legal protections for all.
nodes_flat.content:
*   Behaviour Policy
*   Anti-Bullying Policy
*   Incident Report Forms
*   Relevant legislation, including the Equality Act 2010

nodes_flat.title: 6. Anti-Bullying Policy
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori's Anti-Bullying Policy applies to all (students, staff, parents, visitors) on/off-site, including online. Bullying is defined as repeated, deliberate, hurtful physical, verbal, psychological, or cyber aggression, distinct from 'falling out'. It covers prejudice-based bullying (SEN, sexual orientation, race, religion, disability, gender reassignment) and discriminatory language. The school ensures a safe, supportive environment, preventing bullying via PSHEE, personal pupil knowledge, supervision, and fostering a caring ethos. Report bullying immediately to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head Teacher. Staff (e.g., Lindsay &/or Sheila for records) and parents can report to any staff/Head. Incidents trigger a 'Formal School Warning', 'Incident Report', and 'Families Informed'. Consequences include 'Suspension' (In-School (ISS) or Out-of-School (OSS), 1 day to weeks) or 'Exclusion' (permanent removal/expulsion), a last resort with appeal. Police/Social Services are involved if criminal. Bullying records are reviewed annually at a 'Dashboard Meeting' by Management Team and weekly at staff meetings. Confidentiality is paramount. "No one deserves to be bullied."

nodes_flat.title: 6.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: This policy applies to everyone in the Imagine Montessori School community: students, staff, parents, and visitors. It covers all activities on school grounds, at school-sponsored events, and any online or remote communications involving our community members.
nodes_flat.content: This policy applies to all members of the Imagine Montessori School community, including students, staff, parents, and visitors. It is relevant to all activities occurring within the school grounds, during school-sponsored events, and in any interactions involving members of the school community, including online or remote communications.

nodes_flat.title: Bullying Policy: Terminology and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori defines bullying as deliberate, repeated, hurtful behaviour (physical, verbal, psychological, cyber). A "Victim" is targeted, a "Perpetrator" engages. "Cyber-bullying" is digital. The school ensures a safe, supportive environment, committed to avoiding all bullying. Our Anti-Bullying Policy prevents it by knowing pupils personally, enabling staff to detect distress, and offering free access to the Head/staff. It covers prejudice-based bullying (SEN, sexual orientation, race, religion, disability, gender reassignment) and discriminatory language. PSHEE is part of our education.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Bullying**: Deliberate, repeated, and continuous hurtful behaviour aimed at causing distress to another individual. This includes physical, verbal, psychological, and cyber forms.
*   **Victim**: The individual subjected to bullying behaviour.
*   **Perpetrator**: The individual or group engaging in bullying behaviour.
*   **Cyber-bullying**: Bullying through digital means such as text messages, social media, or emails.
*   **PSHEE**: Personal, Social, Health, and Economic Education.

At Imagine Montessori School, we strongly believe that children have a right to study in a safe, supportive, and secure environment. Incidents of bullying threaten this and cause enormous stress to victims. We are therefore strongly committed to the avoidance of bullying in all its forms.

We believe that the principal means of preventing it is through the maintenance of conditions where bullying is less likely to flourish and is more easily detected. At Imagine Montessori School, all pupils will be known to us personally, and it will therefore be easier for staff to detect signs of possible distress. Moreover, we feel that it will be important that pupils have free and informal access to the Head and other staff. A formal anti-bullying policy will be of utmost importance.

As part of our Anti-Bullying Policy, Imagine Montessori School believes that all children and adults have the right to live in a supportive, caring environment in which pupils feel safe and free from bullying and harassment that may include cyber-bullying and prejudice-based bullying related to special educational needs, sexual orientation, sex, race, religion and belief, gender reassignment, or disability, and the use of discriminatory language. Bullying can occur through several types of anti-social behaviour.

nodes_flat.title: 6.4. Policy Objectives
nodes_flat.summary: Bullying is defined as unacceptable physical, written, or oral abuse/intimidation. The school's policy ensures all members (children, staff, families) understand this, preventing it through awareness and clear reporting procedures. Students are encouraged to report any concerns. The school fosters a caring, non-violent atmosphere, educating via PSHE and assemblies. Students are entitled to feel safe, not be frightened, not be touched inappropriately, and not be teased based on birth, gender, age, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, mental capacity, religion, economic status, or other personal circumstances. This includes protection from cyberbullying (texts, emails, gossip) and any unpleasant comments.
nodes_flat.content:
*   To enable all members of the school community to understand what constitutes bullying, making it clear to children, staff, and families that bullying is completely unacceptable and that children and young people have the right to be protected from physical, written, and oral abuse or intimidation.
*   To prevent bullying by raising awareness with all members of the school community, ensuring they have a clear understanding of what bullying behaviour is.
*   To ensure that all members of the school community know and understand the school policy on bullying and follow the established procedures when bullying is suspected or reported.
*   To ensure that all pupils are confident to report bullying if they feel they are being bullied or they witness bullying towards another student.
*   To involve students actively in developing the anti-bullying ethos in the school.
*   To create and maintain a caring, kind, non-violent, and non-threatening atmosphere in the school and raise the school community awareness through educational opportunities that arise, such as PSHE and citizenship activities, circle time, and assemblies.
*   Students are entitled:
    *   To feel welcome and safe and not be made to feel unhappy by others.
    *   Not to be frightened by others.
    *   Not to be touched in an inappropriate manner.
    *   Not to be teased about birth, gender, age, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, opinion, mental capacity, religion and belief, education, economic status, personality, or any other personal or social circumstance or condition.
    *   Not to be made unhappy by unpleasant comments, notes, letters, text messages, e-mails, phone calls, any form of gossip, or by any other means.
    *   To feel confident to report any concerns.

nodes_flat.title: Rationale and Aims Regarding Bullying
nodes_flat.summary: Our school guarantees a safe, positive environment; bullying is not tolerated. Bullying, whether verbal, physical, or indirect, is a deliberate act causing distress for power, including ostracising, name-calling, threats, or physical assault. All staff, parents, and children share responsibility for prevention. Incidents can be reported to any staff member. We aim to provide a safe, caring environment, promote self-esteem, ensure individuals understand consequences, and have a secure program where all bullying reports are taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon.
nodes_flat.content:
All members of the school have an entitlement to be in an educational environment that is safe, positive, secure, and supportive. Bullying interferes with this.

Bullying can be defined as a deliberate act done to cause distress solely to give a feeling of power, status, or other gratification to the bully. Bullying can range from ostracising, name-calling, teasing, threats, threatening or aggressive language or body posturing, and extortion, through physical intimidation, assault on persons and/or their property. It can be an unresolved single frightening incident that casts a shadow over a child's life, or a series of such incidents.

Staff, parents, and children work together to create a happy, caring, and safe learning environment. Bullying, whether verbal, physical, or indirect, is not tolerated. It is everyone's responsibility to try to prevent occurrences of bullying and to deal with any incidents quickly and effectively.

The victim(s), their friend(s), their parent(s), or other interested people may bring bullying to the attention of any member of staff.

*   To provide a safe, caring environment for the whole school community, especially the children in our care.
*   To promote in everyone a positive attitude and high self-esteem.
*   To help individuals to understand and accept the consequences of their own actions.
*   To develop a programme whereby all incidents of bullying can be reported in a secure atmosphere and will be dealt with fairly and appropriately.
*   To help individuals celebrate and value their differences and to acknowledge and respect the differences of others.
*   To instil in all members of the school community that bullying is unacceptable and that reports of bullying will be taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon.

nodes_flat.title: 6.7. Implementation
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori prioritizes bullying prevention. Staff always listen and act on complaints. Children are encouraged to tell a trusted adult if they feel threatened. The school fosters a caring, cooperative, responsible ethos through explicit PSHE/Citizenship teaching and Montessori moral awareness, starting from Infant Community (Pre-Nursery). Opportunities are provided to discuss friendships and inter-personal skills using drama and role play. Adequate playground supervision is ensured, and bullying is actively discouraged.
nodes_flat.content:
**a. Framework for Preventing Bullying**

Prevention is better than cure, and Imagine Montessori tries to ensure that pupils and adults are educated in anti-bullying strategies. These include:

*   Ensuring that staff always listen to and act on complaints of bullying.
*   Encouraging pupils to tell a trusted adult if they feel threatened in any way.
*   Encouraging the caring and nurturing side of children through explicit teaching of PSHE/Citizenship combined with the Montessori moral awareness which has been developing within the school from when they started in the Infant Community (Pre-Nursery).
*   Working within the school through the curriculum to develop a caring, cooperative, responsible ethos.
*   Providing opportunities to discuss friendships and inter-personal skills, and using drama and role play to meet situations.
*   Ensuring adequate supervision in the playground.
*   Actively discouraging bullying.

nodes_flat.title: 6.8. Definition of Bullying
nodes_flat.summary: Bullying is defined as repeated, continuous, deliberate physical, verbal, or psychological aggression by a student(s) asserting superiority. This hurtful behavior, difficult to defend against, aims to make victims feel threatened, humiliated, and unsafe. Forms include name-calling, violence, intimidation, isolation, ridicule, spreading rumors, and "Cyber-bullying" via technology. It's distinct from 'falling out' or arguing. All bullying cases require utmost confidentiality to protect children's rights.
nodes_flat.content:
Bullying is behaviour which consists of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression from a student or students towards another student and/or these students place themselves in a position of superiority. This behaviour is repeated, continued over time, and deliberate.

Bullying can take many forms but have these elements in common:

*   Hurtful behaviour which is deliberate.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is repeated.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is continuous in time.
*   Hurtful behaviour which can take the form of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression.
*   Hurtful behaviour from which it is difficult to defend oneself; the bully places himself/herself in a position of superiority.

It can take the form of name-calling, threatened violence, actual violence, intimidation, isolation, ridicule, or other action such as spreading unpleasant stories and gossip, sending hurtful or intimidating text messages, e-mails, comments on social media, and telephone calls.

Communications technology (texting, e-mails, messaging, social network sites, Bluetooth) has also enabled a remote, potentially anonymous channel for bullying, popularly referred to as “Cyber-bullying”.

All bullying is designed to make the victim feel threatened, humiliated, and unsafe. Not all negative interactions between students can be deemed as bullying, and the school works hard to ensure that students understand the difference between bullying and ‘falling out’ or arguing.

All members of the School Community are expected to treat all potential bullying cases with utmost confidentiality, ensuring that all children’s rights are protected.

nodes_flat.title: 6.9. Preventing Bullying
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School fosters an anti-bullying ethos. Prevention involves: school rules (circle time, PSHEE), social stories, role-plays, and emotional support. The school's methods include watching for distress, listening/believing/acting on bullying reports, intervening to stop harm, explaining unacceptable behavior, reassuring bullied children, and helping bullies learn alternate strategies. Staff are watchful, promote good behavior, supervise, and report all cases to the Head. Parents are involved and kept informed.
nodes_flat.content:
The school is committed to the ongoing development of an anti-bullying ethos throughout the school with students, staff, and parents.

Imagine Montessori School will use the following methods for helping children to prevent bullying. As and when appropriate, these may include:

*   The school rules through circle time, during Personal, Social, Health Economic (PSHEE) and citizenship activities.
*   Social stories and drawings about positive behaviour and role modelling.
*   Reading stories about positive behaviour expectations and bullying, having them read to a class.
*   Making up role-plays.
*   Providing emotional support to give each child the opportunity to express how they feel.

**Methods**:

*   We will watch for early signs of distress in children.
*   We will listen, believe, and act when a child(ren) communicates that they are being bullied.
*   We will intervene to stop the child who is bullying from harming the other child or children.
*   We will explain to the child doing the bullying why his or her behaviour is unacceptable.
*   We will give reassurance to the child or children who have been bullied.
*   We will help the child who has done the bullying to recognise the impact of his or her actions.
*   We support the child who has done the bullying to learn alternate strategies to manage their behaviour.

**Good Practice for Staff**:

*   Be continually aware, watchful, and available.
*   Promote good behaviour and encourage the care of others.
*   Ensure children are appropriately supervised.
*   Report all cases of bullying to the Head.
*   Parents’ involvement and cooperation can be sought, and parents of children involved will be kept informed.

nodes_flat.title: 6.10. Identifying and Reporting Bullying
nodes_flat.summary:
Students & families must immediately report bullying suspicions to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head Teacher. Staff must observe signs, listen to children, and report concerns to the Head Teacher.

If you feel bullied, tell staff/parents; friends should inform staff. "No one deserves to be bullied." Pupil witnesses should support the victim, encourage them to speak out, or accompany them to a trusted adult.

Staff must reassure pupils, advising details go to their Guide/Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher, who logs all complaints. Parents report issues to any staff, Head of School, or the named person for good behaviour.
nodes_flat.content:
Students and families should report any suspicion of bullying immediately to any of the designated people below:

*   Tutor
*   Key Stage Manager
*   Head teacher

Much of the behaviour which leads to both bullying and intimidation is difficult to observe or can be seen as innocent when it is not. It is therefore essential that staff listen carefully to what children say and watch for any signs that a child may be being intimidated. The staff must always pass on their concern to the other members of staff working with the child and report these concerns to the Head Teacher.

**If you witness any unacceptable behaviour, how you should act:**

1.  **If you feel you are being bullied.**
    Tell someone.
    If you feel you are being bullied, you can tell a member of staff and/or your parent/carer (anyone you feel safe to talk to). It is not weak to do this.
    If you cannot tell anyone at school, you can tell your parents.
    Friends of those who feel bullied should let staff know of bullying incidents.
    No one deserves to be bullied. You have a right for this not to happen to you.

2.  **If you are a pupil and witness bullying behaviour.**
    Support the child who you observed being bullied by offering your friendship and making it clear that, in your opinion, what is happening to them is unkind.
    Encourage them to speak out on their own behalf by confronting the child who was exhibiting the bullying behaviour, or with their permission, confront that child yourself. Accompany the child who you observed being bullied to a trusted adult or suggest that you speak to an adult on their behalf.

3.  **If you are a member of Staff.**
    Reassure and support the pupils involved.
    Advise them that you are required to pass details on to their Guide and/or to the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour (Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher).
    The named person will keep a log of all complaints or incidences of bullying and record the way in which they were dealt with.

4.  **If you are a Parent.**
    Bring the issue to the attention of any member of staff, or to the Head of School, or the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour.

nodes_flat.title: 6.11. Taking Action
nodes_flat.summary: When bullying occurs, a Guide interviews both the bullied child (who provides an 'immediate account of events' and discusses their 'reaction and behaviour') and the child responsible, along with witnesses. All involved write individual accounts. Consequences include a 'Formal School Warning,' which triggers an 'Incident Report' and 'Families Informed' process. An adult witness, Guide, and Head Teacher investigate; both families are contacted ASAP (phone, then written notice) and offered meetings. The warning details the incident and potential 'Suspension' (temporary removal, 1 day to weeks, either In-School (ISS) or Out-of-School (OSS)) or 'Exclusion' (permanent removal/expulsion), a last resort with an appeal process. Exclusion may be reported to the local authority; Police/Social Services are involved only if criminal prosecution is likely. Bullying records are reviewed annually at a 'Dashboard Meeting' by the Management Team and discussed weekly at staff meetings. Parents can contribute to the anti-bullying policy.

nodes_flat.title: Interviewing the child who has been bullied
nodes_flat.summary: When your child has been bullied, their Guide will interview them. They may be asked for a written 'immediate account of events' and to discuss their 'reaction and behaviour' towards the 'child who bullied them'. The 'process for dealing with the incident' will be clearly explained. The Guide can also provide 'further support and advice' if appropriate.
nodes_flat.content:
**Interview the child who has been bullied.**

The child who has experienced the bullying will be interviewed by their Guide, who may ask them to write an immediate account of events. The Guide may also invite the child to discuss their own reaction and behaviour towards the child who bullied them. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them. The Guide may give them further support and advice if deemed appropriate.

nodes_flat.title: Interviewing the child responsible for the behaviour
nodes_flat.summary: Once a Guide confirms a bullying incident, the child identified as responsible for the behavior will be interviewed. Other children related to the incident, such as witnesses, will also be interviewed. Each child is required to write an immediate, individual account of events. The Guide will then clearly explain the specific process for addressing the bullying incident and the next steps to all involved.
nodes_flat.content:
**Interview the child responsible for that behaviour.**

Once the Guide is clear that a bullying incident has occurred, the child who was responsible for the bullying will be interviewed, as will any others related to the incident. Each will be asked to write an immediate account of events. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them.

nodes_flat.title: Actions and Consequences: Formal School Warning
nodes_flat.summary: The "Formal School Warning" section outlines disciplinary sanctions for student misconduct. This formal action, a key consequence, is applied according to specific school policy. Parents should understand the procedures governing student behavior, the types of sanctions listed, and the process for addressing actions that warrant a warning or other disciplinary measures.
nodes_flat.content:
**Actions and Consequences**

The following sanctions may be applied in accordance with the

nodes_flat.title: Actions and Consequences: Suspension
nodes_flat.summary: Our school's Behaviour Policy details actions for bullying. A "Formal School Warning" involves an "Incident Report" and "Families Informed" process. An adult witness, Guide, and Head Teacher investigate. Both bully and victim families are contacted ASAP (phone, then written notice) and offered meetings to discuss findings, actions, and support. The warning, detailing the incident and potential suspension or exclusion, is documented in the student's record. Students learn future expectations and consequences. "Suspension" means temporary removal from school for misbehaviour or policy violation.
nodes_flat.content:
school’s Behaviour Policy:

1.  **Formal School Warning - Incident Report - Families Informed.**
    A formal school warning is a written or verbal notice given to a student who has been involved in bullying. It serves as an official acknowledgment of the behaviour and a warning that further actions will be taken if the behaviour continues.

    *   **Process**:
        *   **Investigation**: Before issuing a formal warning, the adult who witnesses the incident, the Guide, and the Head Teacher will investigate the bullying incident to gather facts, speak with witnesses, and understand the context.
        *   **Families Informed**: Clear and timely communication with the families of both the bully and the victim, ensuring they are aware of the situation, the actions taken by the school, and the support available. The school contacts the families as soon as possible after the incident has been reported and investigated. This is typically done via phone call, followed by a written notice.
        *   The school may arrange meetings with the families to discuss the incident, share the findings of the investigation, and explain the actions taken by the school. For the family of the bully, this may include discussing the formal warning and any further consequences. For the family of the victim, the focus will be on the support and protection measures in place for their child.
        *   The school maintains ongoing communication with the families to keep them informed of any developments. This includes updates on how the situation is being monitored and any further incidents or resolutions.
        *   **Warning Issued**: The school issues the formal warning, which may include a written notice that outlines the incident, suspension, or exclusion following the bullying policy. The warning is documented in the student's record.
        *   **Expectations and Consequences**: The student is informed of the expectations for their future behaviour and the consequences they will face if they do not adhere to these expectations. This might include suspension or exclusion if the behaviour continues.

2.  **Suspension.**
    *   **Definition**: Suspension is a temporary removal of a student from their regular school environment due to misbehaviour or violation of school policies.

nodes_flat.title: Actions and Consequences: Exclusion
nodes_flat.summary: Suspensions (1 day to weeks) serve as a consequence, reflection time, and warning. They can be In-School Suspension (ISS), isolating the student on campus, or Out-of-School Suspension (OSS), barring them from school. Exclusion, also called expulsion or permanent exclusion, is the permanent removal of a student from school.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Duration**: Suspensions can vary from a day to a few days or weeks, depending on the severity of the behaviour. The student is expected to return to school after the suspension period ends.
    *   **Purpose**: The main goal of suspension is to provide a consequence for the student's actions while also giving them time to reflect on their behaviour. It also serves as a warning to the student and others that certain behaviours are unacceptable.
    *   **Types**:
        *   In-School Suspension: The student remains at school but is isolated from their peers and regular activities.
        *   Out-of-School Suspension: The student is not allowed to attend school or participate in any school-related activities during the suspension period.

3.  **Exclusion.**
    *   **Definition**: Exclusion, also known as expulsion or permanent exclusion, is the permanent removal of a student from the school.

nodes_flat.title: Reporting and Policy Review
nodes_flat.summary: Exclusion is indefinite, meaning a child cannot return to school. It's a last resort for severe/repeated behavior, risk to others, or rule failure, involving formal meetings and an appeal process. This may be reported to the local authority. Police/Social Services are involved only if criminal prosecution is likely; the school prefers internal resolution. Staff discuss this policy annually. Parents can contribute to anti-bullying policy. Bullying records are reviewed annually by the Management Team at their 'Dashboard Meeting'. Incidents are discussed weekly at staff meetings and tackled within the year group/class.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Duration**: Exclusion is indefinite and means that the student is no longer allowed to attend the school from which they were excluded. The student would need to seek education elsewhere.
    *   **Purpose**: Exclusion is used as a last resort when a student's behaviour is so severe or repeated that the school believes they can no longer be a part of the school community. This might be due to actions that pose a significant risk to the safety or well-being of others or a repeated failure to adhere to school rules despite previous interventions.
    *   **Process**: Exclusion usually involves a formal process, including meetings with the student, parents or guardians, and school administrators. There may be an appeal process where the decision can be reviewed.

This may be reported to the local authority.

These are minimum sanctions. In very serious cases, it may be necessary to make a report to the Police or Social Services. However, it is the policy of the school to attempt to resolve such issues internally using our own disciplinary sanctions, unless the matter is of such gravity that a criminal prosecution is likely.

The staff will discuss this policy and its implications on their practice at least once every year. Where possible, parents will be invited to contribute to the school’s policy and procedures to prevent bullying. The records of bullying offences will be reviewed by the Management Team once a year at their ‘Dashboard Meeting’ at the end of each school year.

In parallel, we will tackle the issue within the year group/class.

Each incident will be discussed at the weekly staff meeting to inform the other members of staff about how it has been addressed and prevent further incidents.

nodes_flat.title: 6.12. Record of the Incident
nodes_flat.summary: Welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila) record incident details. The Guide consults the named behaviour support person to determine the best course of action. This may involve a formal bullying warning, clearly defining the incident and what constitutes continued bullying (e.g., hurtful discussion), or strategies to foster kindness between children. The Anti-Bullying Policy is reviewed annually by the Leadership Team. Staff discuss it during professional development. Bullying records are analyzed for patterns, trends, and intervention effectiveness. Key reference documents include the school's Behaviour & Child Protection Policies, Local Authority Guidelines, UNCRC, National Anti-Bullying Alliance resources, and the PSHEE/Citizenship Curriculum.
nodes_flat.content:
Details of the incident will be recorded by the welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila). The Guide will discuss with the named person for behaviour support what the appropriate and most helpful course of action is.

This may include:

*   A formal bullying warning where it is made clear what the bullying incident was and what would constitute a continuation of the bullying, including discussion of the incident or process in a hurtful way.
*   Clear strategies on what work would help both children (or groups of children) to be kind and loving towards each other.

**Monitoring and Review**

The Anti-Bullying Policy will be reviewed annually by the school’s Leadership Team.

Staff members will discuss the policy during designated professional development sessions to ensure consistency and effectiveness in its application. Records of bullying incidents will be analyzed for patterns, trends, and effectiveness of interventions.

**Reference Documents**

*   Imagine Montessori School Behaviour Policy
*   Imagine Montessori School Child Protection Policy
*   Local Authority Guidelines on Anti-Bullying Practices
*   United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC)
*   National Anti-Bullying Alliance Resources
*   School’s PSHEE and Citizenship Curriculum Framework

nodes_flat.title: 7. Drug and Alcohol Policy
nodes_flat.summary: The school's Drug and Alcohol Policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and contractors on school premises, off-site events, and travel. It covers legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed/OTC drugs, illegal substances (e.g., cannabis), and "Legal Highs" (NPS). "Substance Misuse" means negative health/behavior impact; "Intoxicated" means impaired functioning. IMS strictly prohibits pupils from possessing, consuming, buying, selling, or exchanging drugs/substances, including smoking/vaping. Prescription medicine requires Head of Primary/Secondary agreement. Spanish law (Ley 42/2010) bans smoking on school premises. Staff must not be under the influence. Drug education is integrated into PSHE (Y7-13), covering risks, laws, and healthy choices, with Policia Nacional involvement. For pupil incidents, medical aid is first, followed by Behaviour/Exclusion Policies (including searches by Headteachers with 2 staff). Police involvement is at the Headteacher's discretion. Parents will be informed of incidents unless it risks the child. Parents under influence on premises will be asked to leave; refusal leads to police involvement. Staff receive mandatory drug awareness training. The Secondary Headteacher oversees the policy, reviewed annually. Parents can inquire about "pastoral care," "student well-being," "safeguarding," or "incident protocols

nodes_flat.title: Scope and Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: This Drug and Alcohol Policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and contractors, covering school premises, off-site events, and travel. It addresses legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed/over-the-counter drugs, and illegal substances (e.g., cannabis), plus "Legal Highs" (NPS). The policy aims for drug prevention, education, and incident response, fostering a whole-school approach to well-being. Teachers will be skilled, and pupils receive accurate information and support. "Substance Misuse" means negative health/behavior impact; "Intoxicated" means impaired functioning.
nodes_flat.content:
This policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and third-party contractors associated with the school. It encompasses all activities conducted on school premises, during school-related events off-site, and while pupils are travelling to and from school. The policy extends to incidents involving legal and illegal substances, including alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents, prescribed and over-the-counter drugs, and New Psychoactive Substances (NPS).

The aim of this Drug and Alcohol Policy is to acknowledge and clarify the school’s role in drug prevention and education, and ensure it is appropriate to meet pupils’ needs. This policy provides information about drug education, as well as procedures to respond to any drug-related incident.

This policy aims to ensure that the approach taken on the issue of drugs is a whole-school one and is part of our commitment to, and concern for, the overall health and well-being of the whole school community. Teachers will need to be confident and skilled to teach drug education, and pupils need to receive up-to-date, relevant, and accurate information, as well as support.

This policy should be read in conjunction with the policies and documents listed on the back page of this document.

*   **Drugs**: Includes legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed and over-the-counter medicines, and illegal substances (cannabis, ecstasy, amphetamines, heroin, cocaine, LSD, etc.).
*   **NPS**: New Psychoactive Substances, often referred to as "Legal Highs."
*   **Substance Misuse**: The use of any drug in a way that negatively impacts health, behaviour, or performance.
*   **Intoxicated**: Impaired by drugs or alcohol to a level that affects normal functioning.

nodes_flat.title: Statutory Duties and Application of Policy
nodes_flat.summary: Schools have a statutory duty to promote pupil wellbeing and prevent drug misuse (pastoral care). The DfE advises a Drug Policy for incidents. Spanish law (Ley 28/2005, 42/2010) bans smoking in/around schools. The EYFS (2021, para 3.57) prohibits smoking, vaping, or e-cigarettes on premises when children are present, including offsite trips. Staff must not be under the influence of alcohol or other substances. This policy applies to all Imagine pupils, staff, parents, and third parties on school property, educational visits, off-site events, and public transport to/from school. It also covers all staff, visitors, and contractors. "Adults" are employees/supervisors.
nodes_flat.content:
Schools have a statutory duty to promote pupils’ wellbeing and hence have a clear role in preventing drug misuse as part of their pastoral care. Although there is no statutory requirement to have a Drug Policy, it is the advice of the DfE for schools to have a clear procedure for managing any incidents related to drugs/tobacco or alcohol misuse. It is essential to protect staff, parent/carer(s), children, and young people.

Spanish legislation (Ley 28/2005 de 26 de diciembre, as modified by Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre) prohibits smoking in and around educational establishments. The Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021) requires the following (para 3.57): ‘Providers must not allow smoking, vaping, or the use of e-cigarettes in or on the premises when children are present or about to be present.’ This includes offsite trips and visits. Furthermore, practitioners must not be under the influence of alcohol or any other substances which may affect their ability to care for children, as per their Code of Conduct.

*   This policy applies to all pupils on roll at the school. It applies on school property, when on educational visits, and when off-site at school-related events to ALL Imagine students, staff, parents, and third parties. This policy applies when pupils are travelling to and from school on public transport. Any responsibilities of ‘adults’ refers to employees and others acting in a supervisory role with pupils.
*   This policy also applies to all staff, visitors, and third-party contractors.

nodes_flat.title: 7.5. The School’s Stance on Drugs, Health and the Needs of Pupils
nodes_flat.summary: IMS strictly prohibits pupils from possessing, consuming, buying, selling, or exchanging drugs/substances on school grounds, during school hours, or on school visits. This includes a no smoking/vaping/e-cigarette policy. Prescription medicine use is permitted only with Head of Primary or Secondary agreement. Spanish law (Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre) bans smoking on school premises and selling alcohol in schools.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Possession**: It is inappropriate and unacceptable for pupils to bring the above drugs into school, have them on their person, consume them before, during, and after school, and on the way to or from school, including on school visits, etc. IMS has a strict no smoking policy (including no vaping/e-cigarettes) on school sites. The drugs/substances covered by this policy are not to be purchased, sold, or otherwise exchanged during the school day or while pupils are on school visits.
*   **Use**: The school states that the use of drugs in school, during the school day, while travelling to/from school, or on school trips is inappropriate. Individual exceptions may be made for pupils who require prescription medicines, where appropriate and with the agreement of the Head of Primary or Secondary.
*   Legislation in Spain (*Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre*) states that it is illegal to smoke on school premises. In Spain, it is also illegal to supply or sell alcoholic drinks in schools.

nodes_flat.title: 7.6. Policy Framework
nodes_flat.summary:
Our school's drug education, integrated into the PSHE programme, aims to empower pupils to make healthy, informed choices. It's based on British, national, and local guidelines, tailored to age.

For Years 7-9, the curriculum covers legal and illegal substances (alcohol, volatile substances, tobacco, cannabis), related laws, managing peer influence, and personal/social risks, including benefits of not drinking/smoking (and avoiding second-hand smoke). They learn about safe medicine use, 'experimental' use risks, 'dependence,' 'addiction,' and accessing local health services.

Years 10-11 delve into long-term health, mental/emotional well-being consequences, and wider risks to career, relationships, and future lifestyle. They further understand 'habit,' 'dependence,' and 'addiction.'

Years 12-13 focus on managing alcohol/drug use for health, decision-making, and personal safety, evaluating impacts on road safety, workplace safety, legal safety, reputation, and career. Specific topics include drink spiking, looking out for friends, and using licensed taxis.

We invite visitors like Policia Nacional to support our teacher-led programm
nodes_flat.content:
**a. Drugs Education**

Drug education in our school aims to enable pupils to make healthy informed choices by increasing their knowledge, exploring a range of attitudes towards drug use, and developing and practising decision-making skills. The programme we follow will be based on British, national, and local guidelines for good practice and be appropriate to the age and experience of our pupils. Teaching will be based on an understanding that a variety of approaches should be used in order to meet the differing needs and learning styles of pupils.

We will provide all pupils with drug education as an integral part of our Personal, Social, Health and Economic (PSHE) programme, which includes:

**b. Content and Delivery**

**Year 7 - 9**

*   The positive and negative roles played by drugs in society (including alcohol).
*   Information about legal and illegal substances, including alcohol, volatile substances, tobacco, and cannabis, and the law relating to their supply, use, and misuse.
*   To recognise and manage different influences on their decisions about the use of substances (including clarifying and challenging their own perceptions, values, and beliefs), including managing peer influence.
*   The personal and social risks and consequences of substance use and misuse, including the benefits of not drinking alcohol (or delaying the age at which to start) and the benefits of not smoking, including not harming others with second-hand smoke.
*   The safe use of prescribed and over-the-counter medicines.
*   The risks and consequences of ‘experimental’ and ‘occasional’ substance use and the terms ‘dependence’ and ‘addiction’.
*   About how to access local health services.
*   About cancer and cancer prevention, including healthy lifestyles, acknowledging that childhood cancers are rarely caused by lifestyle choices.

**Year 10 & 11 - 13**

*   The short and long-term consequences of substance use and misuse for the health and mental and emotional well-being of individuals, families, and communities, including the health risks related to second-hand smoke.
*   Understand the terms ‘habit’, ‘dependence’, and ‘addiction’ in relation to substance use and to whom to talk if they have concerns.
*   The wider risks and consequences of legal and illegal substance use, including on their personal safety, career, relationships, and future lifestyle.

**Year 12 & 13**

*   How to manage alcohol and drug use in relation to immediate and long-term health.
*   Understand alcohol and drug use can affect decision-making and personal safety.
*   Evaluate the impact of drugs and alcohol and their use on road safety, workplace safety, legal safety, reputation, and career.
*   Manage personal safety off-line, including when socialising (including meeting someone in person for the first time whom they met online, drink spiking, looking out for friends) and travelling (especially cycle safety, young driver safety, and passenger safety, including the risks of being a passenger with an intoxicated driver, and using only licensed taxis).

**c. Use of Visitors and Outside Speakers**

We invite visitors such as the Policia Nacional to support our planned teacher-led programme of education, in line with British, national, and local guidance. We are careful to negotiate the use of visitors in line with the protocol for using visitors in IMS, so that their contribution fits our needs and they are clear how their input fits into our planned programme. Teachers are always present when visitors are working with our pupils.

nodes_flat.title: 7.7. Staff Support and Training
nodes_flat.summary: The school provides mandatory drug awareness training for all new staff during induction. Staff involved in drug education receive this training on an ongoing basis to ensure up-to-date knowledge and practices.
nodes_flat.content: The school is committed to providing drug awareness training in the induction of all staff working at the school and on an ongoing basis for staff involved in drugs education.

nodes_flat.title: Incident Management and Police Involvement
nodes_flat.summary: For pupil drug incidents (possession, use, selling), medical aid (7.3) is first. Behaviour/Exclusion Policies apply, including searches and sanctions. Police involvement is at the Headteacher's discretion, consulting the Director, who is informed of all incidents. No legal obligation to call police. Parents under drug/alcohol influence on school premises will be asked to leave; child withheld for safety if collecting. Refusal to leave results in police being called. Serious concerns may lead to a parent site ban. See Appendices A & B.
nodes_flat.content:
*   Where there is evidence or suspicion that a pupil has drugs in their possession, has used drugs, or has passed on/sold drugs to another person, the school will initially seek medical attention for the child if needed, as per 7.3.
*   The school will follow its Behaviour and Exclusion Policies and may also use its power to search a pupil. Sanctions will be applied as outlined in the Behaviour and Exclusion Policies.
*   Where it is believed that a parent or carer is under the influence of drugs (including alcohol) on the school premises, they will be asked to leave. If they are due to collect their child, then they will be asked to make alternative arrangements, and the child will be withheld to ensure their safety. If a parent or carer refuses to leave, the Headteacher or their representative should call the police. Where there are serious or ongoing concerns about the adult’s presentation and drug use, the school may consider banning a parent from entering the school site.
*   See Appendix B – Responding to incidents involving drugs.

*   While there is no legal obligation to inform the police, they may be involved at the discretion of the Headteacher in consultation with the Director. The school will consider each incident individually and will employ a range of responses to deal with individual incidents. The Headteachers will inform the Director in relation to all drug-related incidents.
*   See Appendix A for guidance on drug situations linked to medical emergencies.

nodes_flat.title: 7.10. The Needs of Pupils
nodes_flat.summary: The school consistently exercises its pastoral responsibilities, ensuring pupils always access appropriate support and guidance. Parents can inquire about specific "pastoral care" or "student well-being" provisions for their child.
nodes_flat.content: The school will exercise its pastoral responsibilities and always look to support pupils and ensure that pupils have access to appropriate support and guidance.

nodes_flat.title: 7.11. Information Sharing
nodes_flat.summary: If the school has concerns about a child’s safety, the school will share information with law enforcement agencies and safeguarding agencies. This process is outlined in the Safeguarding Policy and involves working in partnership with external agencies to ensure child protection.
nodes_flat.content: If the school has concerns about a child’s safety, the school will share information with law enforcement agencies and safeguarding agencies as outlined in the Safeguarding Policy. This includes working in partnership with external agencies.

nodes_flat.title: 7.12. Involvement of Parent/Carer(s)
nodes_flat.summary: Parents/carers will be informed by the school of any drug-related incidents involving their child. This notification is mandatory unless, in very rare circumstances, it would heighten the risk to the child.
nodes_flat.content: The school will inform parents and carers of any drug-related incidents involving their own child, unless in very rare circumstances this would heighten the risk to the child.

nodes_flat.title: 7.13. Staff Conduct and Drug Use
nodes_flat.summary: All staff must follow the Staff Code of Conduct. Breaching policy leads to disciplinary action under the IMS disciplinary policy and other HR procedures.
nodes_flat.content: All staff are required to adhere to the Staff Code of Conduct. Staff are subject to the IMS disciplinary policy and other HR procedures should they breach policy.

nodes_flat.title: 7.14. The Role of the Headteacher
nodes_flat.summary: The Secondary Headteacher is responsible for this policy, ensuring staff training. IMS premises cannot be used for drug production/supply; suspected sales are reported to police. Staff cannot promise confidentiality if significant harm is a risk, following the Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy. Parental substance misuse is considered; the Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator liaises with agencies. Suspected controlled drugs require a second adult witness. Headteachers have statutory power to search pupils/possessions (without consent, reasonable grounds, 2 staff). The Leadership Team reviews policies annually or after incidents/legislative changes, communicating updates to parents. Key reference documents include the Child Protection Policy, EYFS (2021), Spanish laws (Ley 28/2005, Ley 42/2010), and DfE guidance. Parents can inquire about safeguarding or incident protocols.
nodes_flat.content:
*   The Secondary Headteacher takes overall responsibility for the implementation of this policy.
*   The Secondary Headteacher will ensure that all staff dealing with substance issues are adequately trained and supported.
*   IMS cannot knowingly allow premises to be used for the production or supply of any controlled drug. Where it is suspected that substances are sold on the premises, details of those involved, together with as much information as possible, will be automatically passed to the police.

nodes_flat.title: 7.15. Specific Procedures
nodes_flat.summary: Regarding information, staff cannot promise confidentiality to your child, especially if there's a risk of significant harm, and must follow the Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy. Parental substance misuse isn't automatically significant harm but is considered; the Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator will liaise with agencies if needed. For suspected controlled drugs, a second adult witness must be present. Headteachers have statutory power to search pupils/possessions without consent for prohibited items (with reasonable grounds), requiring 2 staff members to be present.
nodes_flat.content:
**Dealing with Information and Confidentiality**

*   The school Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy and Procedures must be followed. Staff cannot and should not promise confidentiality to the pupil.
*   The following questions should also be considered:
    *   How serious is the situation?
    *   What immediate and significant risk does the young person face?
    *   What implications (both positive and negative) could keeping a confidence have?
    *   Could there be a transfer of risk to other children?
    *   Could significant harm result from keeping the young person’s disclosure confidential?

**Parental Substance Misuse**

*   Parental substance misuse has the potential to impact negatively on outcomes for children and young people.
*   Substance use is not a reason for considering a child to be suffering or at risk of suffering significant harm, although it may be a contributing factor.
*   The Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator or Head will liaise with the police and/or external agencies where required.

**Controlled Drugs (including New Psychoactive Substances)**

In taking temporary possession of suspected controlled drugs, the school will:

*   Ensure that a second adult witness is present throughout.

**Searching, Screening, and Confiscations**

Headteachers and staff authorised by them have a statutory power to search pupils or their possessions without consent where they have reasonable grounds for suspecting that the pupil may have a prohibited item. Searches must be done with 2 members of staff present.

nodes_flat.title: 7.16. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: Our 'Monitoring and Review' process keeps policies up-to-date. The 'Leadership Team' reviews them annually, or after 'significant incidents' or 'legislative changes'. All 'updates' are communicated to 'stakeholders' (including parents) to ensure the policy remains 'relevant and effective' for everyone.
nodes_flat.content:
The Leadership Team will review the policy annually or following significant incidents or legislative changes.

Updates will be communicated to all stakeholders, ensuring the policy remains relevant and effective.

nodes_flat.title: 7.17. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: Reference documents detail policies for child safety, behaviour, and staff conduct. These include the Child Protection Policy, Behaviour Policy, and Code of Conduct. The Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021) outlines legal requirements for children's learning and welfare. Spanish laws Ley 28/2005 (26 Dec) and Ley 42/2010 (30 Dec) are cited, likely concerning public health. Department for Education guidance on managing drug-related incidents is also included. Parents can inquire about safeguarding, EYFS standards, or incident protocols.
nodes_flat.content:
*   Child Protection Policy
*   Behaviour Policy
*   Code of Conduct Document
*   Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021)
*   Ley 28/2005 de 26 de diciembre
*   Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre
*   Department for Education Guidance on Managing Drug-Related Incidents

nodes_flat.title: 8. Weapons and Dangerous Items Policy
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School (IMS) enforces a "Zero Tolerance" policy on weapons, applying to all students, staff, parents, and visitors on/off-site. A "weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (real, replica, toy), bladed objects (knives, scissors), explosives, laser pens, and tools (e.g., screwdrivers) if used offensively. A "Prohibited Item" is any object posing a school safety risk. Staff can search with consent; Headteachers/staff have statutory power to search for "prohibited items" without consent if they have "reasonable grounds." Consequences for incidents, judged individually, include "fixed-term exclusion," "permanent exclusion," or a "Safety Plan" for pupil support. IMS prioritizes safety, uses a PSHE curriculum to educate on dangers, and expects open communication with parents. The policy is reviewed annually.

nodes_flat.title: 8.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Imagine Montessori School's (IMS) weapons policy applies to all students, staff, parents, visitors, and individuals on the IMS site or at school events (on/off-site). It details preventative measures, actions, and procedures for addressing weapons presence/use, alongside safety and support protocols for the entire community.
nodes_flat.content: This policy applies to all students, staff, parents, visitors, and any other individuals accessing the Imagine Montessori School (IMS) site. It outlines the preventative measures, actions, and procedures in place to address the presence or use of weapons within the school grounds, as well as the support and safety measures provided to our community in such situations. This policy covers all areas of the school premises, during school hours, and at any school-organized events or activities, whether on or off-site.

nodes_flat.title: 8.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: "Weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (real, replica, toy), bladed objects (knives, scissors), explosives (fireworks), aerosol sprays, laser pens, martial arts equipment (nunchaku), and tools (screwdrivers) if used offensively. A "Prohibited Item" is any object posing a school safety risk. A "Safety Plan" is a structured intervention for a pupil after a weapon incident, ensuring safety and promoting positive behavior.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Weapon**: For the purposes of this policy, a weapon includes but is not limited to:
    *   Firearms, including starting pistols, air guns, pellet guns, BB guns, and replicas or toy guns.
    *   Bladed objects such as knives, craft knives, scissors, and razor blades.
    *   Explosives, including fireworks.
    *   Other objects like aerosol sprays, lighters, matches, laser pens, and martial arts equipment (e.g., nunchaku, death stars).
    *   Tools such as screwdrivers, hammers, and chisels that can be offensively used.
*   **Prohibited Item**: Any object identified under this policy that poses a potential risk to the safety and well-being of the school community.
*   **Safety Plan**: A structured intervention created for a pupil following a weapon-related incident, aimed at ensuring their safety and the safety of others while promoting positive behaviour and support.

nodes_flat.title: 8.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: IMS prioritizes safety with a "Zero Tolerance" policy: weapons are strictly prohibited. Staff take immediate action (searches, confiscation, reporting to authorities) if a weapon is suspected or found. A strong safeguarding culture and PSHE curriculum educate students on dangers. Incidents are handled individually, considering intent and risk, with actions from exclusion to safety planning, always prioritizing overall safety. Open communication with parents is key.
nodes_flat.content:
1.  **Commitment to Safety**: At IMS, the safety and well-being of all community members is a top priority. Immediate action will always be taken to reduce risks and ensure a secure environment for everyone on-site.
2.  **Preventative Culture**:
    *   IMS promotes a strong safeguarding culture where staff, students, and parents are vigilant and proactive in addressing potential risks.
    *   The Personal, Social, Health, and Economic (PSHE) curriculum educates students on the dangers and legal implications of carrying or using weapons.
    *   Open communication between the school and parents is essential to reinforce a shared responsibility in addressing safety concerns.
3.  **Responsiveness and Individual Context**:
    *   Every incident involving a weapon will be handled based on the individual circumstances, intent, and level of risk posed.
    *   Decisions will prioritize the safety of all while considering the needs of the pupil involved, with actions ranging from exclusion to safety planning and support.
4.  **Zero Tolerance**:
    *   Weapons of any kind are strictly prohibited on school premises.
    *   Staff will take immediate action upon suspicion or discovery of a weapon, including searches, confiscation, and necessary reporting to authorities.

nodes_flat.title: 8.4. Purpose and Aim
nodes_flat.summary: Our school's policy aims to prevent weapons on site and details direct actions for incidents, always prioritizing student safety. A "weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (e.g., toy guns, air guns), bladed objects (e.g., knives, scissors), explosives (e.g., fireworks, aerosol sprays), martial arts items, and tools (e.g., screwdrivers, hammers) if used offensively. Preventative strategies involve ALL staff understanding this policy, fostering a strong safeguarding culture, and using our PSHE curriculum to educate pupils on weapon risks and legal implications. Parents must be aware of this stringent policy, support senior leaders' decisions, and remain vigilant regarding their children's behavior.
nodes_flat.content:
At Imagine Montessori School, the purpose of this policy guidance is to clearly set out the preventative actions in place to prevent the use of any weapons on our site. This policy guidance will also show the direct actions a school will take around the use of weapons on site, taking into account the full context around each and every incident.

At IMS, we are committed to the safety and well-being of all of our community and will always take immediate action to reduce the risk of harm to any individual on our school site.

**What is a weapon?**

For the purpose of this Policy, a “weapon” is: a firearm of any description, including starting pistols, air guns, and any type of replica or toy gun, pellet guns, or BB guns; knives, including all variations of bladed objects, i.e., pocket knives, craft knives, scissors, etc.; explosives, including fireworks, aerosol sprays, lighters, matches; laser pens or other objects, even if manufactured for a non-violent purpose, but have a potentially violent use, i.e., the purpose of keeping or carrying the object is for use, or threat of use, as a weapon. Nunchucks, death stars, and other martial arts objects. Screwdrivers, hammers, chisels, and any tool that could be offensively used. Razors, razor blades, or chains.

**Preventative Strategies**

ALL staff must read and understand this policy and the expectations of keeping children safe. Creating a safeguarding culture so that pupils and staff can feel safe requires everyone to agree to and work within the school’s ethos and culture of vigilance and positively contribute to the sharing of information where there may be risk. By creating a strong culture of safeguarding within the school, all individuals accessing the site should feel safe to raise concerns, seek support, and for a seamless and consistent response to incidents to be followed. Effective use of the curriculum, having an effective curriculum in place via PSHE, should support pupils in understanding the risks around the use of weapons, the legal implications of carrying weapons, and the school’s response to such incidents. Parents need to be aware of the school’s stringent policy around weapons and support the decisions made by senior leaders in the event of an incident. Parents should always be vigilant of the behaviours of their own children and seek support and intervention when necessary.

nodes_flat.title: 8.5. Searching & Confiscation
nodes_flat.summary: School staff can search your child with consent for any item. Without consent, Headteachers/staff have statutory power to search for "prohibited items" if they have "reasonable grounds." They can seize "prohibited items" or anything "harmful/detrimental to school discipline." Consequences depend on risk/intent: "fixed-term exclusion" (possibly with a "disciplinary hearing"), "permanent exclusion," or a "safety plan" upon return. A "safety plan" supports children, especially after a "weapon" incident, involving parents to manage risks and ensure safety. Each case is judged individually.
nodes_flat.content:
1.  **Searching**
    School staff can search a pupil for any item if the pupil agrees. Headteachers and staff have a statutory power to search pupils or their possessions, without consent, where they have reasonable grounds for suspecting that the pupil may have a prohibited item.

2.  **Confiscating**
    School staff can seize any prohibited item found as a result of a search. They can also seize any item they consider harmful or detrimental to school discipline.

3.  **Deciding on the Next Course of Action**
    From the outcome of the investigation, the school must decide on the level of risk the pupil poses in school. If the risk is clear and the intent is obvious, then a fixed-term exclusion and a disciplinary hearing may follow, or the decision to permanently exclude may be made (following necessary protocols, reporting to the police, social services, etc.). However, depending on the intent, the age of the child, and also the circumstances surrounding the incident or the contextual issues, it may be that a fixed-term exclusion is given, followed by a risk assessment/safety plan put in place following the pupil’s return to school.

    **Returning to School/Safety Planning**
    Safety planning is a positive way of supporting a child who may benefit from a planned approach to support or intervention following an incident where a weapon has been brought to school. Safety plans support the child by considering the behaviour behind the use of a weapon and planning ways to manage any identified risks/triggers and seek support from adults and peers. They are inclusive of parents and staff and are a planned intervention to support young people in feeling secure in the school/setting, helping young people identify behaviours that may leave them feeling anxious or at risk, and have strategies they can apply to keep themselves feeling safe.

    Each case will be judged on an individual basis.

nodes_flat.title: 8.6. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The policy's effectiveness is reviewed annually or after any weapons incident. These reviews assess the policy's clarity, preventative measures, incident responses, and gather feedback from staff, students, and parents. Amendments will be made as needed to address emerging risks and improve safeguarding practices.
nodes_flat.content:
The effectiveness of this policy will be reviewed annually or following any incident involving weapons, whichever occurs first. Reviews will assess:

*   The clarity and accessibility of the policy for all stakeholders.
*   The effectiveness of preventative measures and curriculum strategies.
*   The appropriateness and consistency of responses to incidents.
*   Feedback from staff, students, and parents on the policy's implementation.

Amendments will be made as necessary to address emerging risks and improve safeguarding practices.

nodes_flat.title: 8.7. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: For full understanding, this policy must be read with the Child Protection and Safeguarding Document, IMS Behaviour Policy, and relevant legal frameworks like the Education Act 1996 and Children Act 1989. It also incorporates local authority safeguarding guidelines.
nodes_flat.content:
This policy should be read in conjunction with:

*   Child Protection and Safeguarding Document.
*   IMS Behaviour Policy.
*   Relevant legal frameworks, including the Education Act 1996 and the Children Act 1989.
*   Local authority safeguarding guidelines.

nodes_flat.title: 9. External Examinations Policies
nodes_flat.summary:
External exam policies detail "exam malpractice" (cheating) for GCSE/A-Levels, covering unauthorised items (phones, smartwatches), plagiarism, collusion, and undeclared AI tools (ChatGPT). A 5-step process handles suspected cases, leading to serious consequences: lost marks, disqualification, UCAS reports, and notification to AQA/Cambridge. Students must follow JCQ rules and declare AI use.

Parents can appeal internal assessment marks to the Exams Officer within 5 school days; a Review Panel gives a final decision within 10. "Enquiries About Results" (EARs) for external exams require written consent and submission within 2-3 weeks of results, with the crucial note that marks can decrease. Responsible AI use (planning/brainstorming *only if permitted* and *acknowledged*) is taught; misuse leads to an Academic Misconduct Panel and potential disqualification. All policies are reviewed annually and accessible on Phidias.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1. Malpractice Policy – GCSE and A-Level Exams
nodes_flat.summary: The Malpractice Policy defines "exam malpractice" (cheating) as compromising assessment integrity, e.g., using unauthorised items (phones, smartwatches), plagiarism, collusion, or undeclared AI tools (ChatGPT). Suspected cases follow a 5-step process: investigation, evidence, report to exam boards (AQA, Cambridge), interview, and sanctions. Consequences are serious: warnings, lost marks, disqualification, UCAS reports. Students must follow JCQ rules, avoid unauthorised items, and declare AI use. Parents can find the full policy on Phidias. It's reviewed annually, adhering to JCQ guidelines.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Students are informed about 'exam malpractice' (cheating), its definition, and the specific consequences for violations. The policy guides them on maintaining academic integrity in all assessments and examinations, ensuring awareness of fair conduct rules and 'what happens if' standards are broken.
nodes_flat.content: This policy ensures that all students are aware of what constitutes exam malpractice, the consequences of engaging in it, and how to maintain the highest standards of integrity during all assessments and examinations.

nodes_flat.title: Malpractice in Assessments and Procedures for Dealing with Suspected Cases
nodes_flat.summary: Malpractice is any act compromising assessment integrity or giving unfair advantage. Student examples include unauthorised materials (phones, smartwatches), copying, plagiarism, collusion, and using AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) without explicit permission. Submitting AI-generated content (essays, code) or failing to declare AI use is malpractice. Suspected cases follow a 5-step process: Initial Investigation (Exams Officer/teacher), Evidence Collection, Formal Report to exam boards (e.g., AQA, Cambridge), Candidate Interview, and Outcome/Sanctions by school/board. Parents should understand these rules.
nodes_flat.content:
Malpractice refers to any act that compromises the integrity of an assessment or examination. This includes behaviour by a student, teacher, or other individual that gives an unfair advantage or affects the result of the exam.

Examples of student malpractice include, but are not limited to:

*   Bringing unauthorised materials into the exam room (e.g., mobile phones, notes, smartwatches).
*   Copying from another student or allowing another student to copy.
*   Collusion (working with others on work that must be completed individually).
*   Plagiarism (submitting someone else’s work as your own).
*   Using AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) to generate assessable content unless explicitly permitted.
*   Impersonating another candidate or having someone else take the exam on your behalf.
*   Disruptive behaviour in or near the exam room.
*   Attempting to communicate with others during an exam.
*   Altering or forging coursework or controlled assessments after submission.

*   AI-generated content (e.g., essays, code, research) must not be submitted for assessment unless specifically allowed.
*   Use of translation apps, grammar checkers, or idea-generation tools must follow teacher guidelines.
*   Failure to declare use of AI tools, or passing AI-generated work off as your own, is considered malpractice.

If a student is suspected of malpractice, the following steps will be taken:

1.  Initial Investigation by the Exams Officer or relevant teacher.
2.  Collection of Evidence – this may include witness statements, copies of exam papers, plagiarism reports, etc.
3.  Formal Report will be submitted to the relevant exam board (e.g., AQA, Cambridge) if necessary.
4.  Candidate Interview – the student will be given a chance to respond to the allegation.
5.  Outcome and Sanctions – decisions may be made by the school or by the exam board, depending on the severity and nature of the case.

nodes_flat.title: Malpractice: Consequences and Student Responsibilities
nodes_flat.summary: Malpractice consequences are serious: warnings, lost marks, disqualification from subjects or all exams, even UCAS reports. Exam boards make final decisions, not schools. To avoid this, students must follow JCQ rules, be on time with equipment, bring no unauthorized items, submit original coursework/NEAs, and follow teacher guidance on tech/AI, asking if unsure.
nodes_flat.content:
Consequences of malpractice can be serious and may include:

*   A warning.
*   Loss of marks for a component or subject.
*   Disqualification from the subject.
*   Disqualification from all qualifications for that exam series.
*   Being reported to UCAS or other institutions (in severe cases).

These decisions are made by the exam board, not the school, and are final.

To avoid malpractice, students must:

*   Follow all JCQ (Joint Council for Qualifications) exam regulations.
*   Attend exams on time and with the correct equipment.
*   Not bring unauthorised materials into the exam room.
*   Submit their own, original work for coursework and NEAs.
*   Follow teacher guidance regarding the appropriate use of technology and AI. Ask if unsure whether something is permitted.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.8. Communication
nodes_flat.summary: This policy will be shared with students during assemblies, tutor time, and class time. Parents can find the full policy document on Phidias. This ensures students are informed directly and parents have easy access to review the policy details.
nodes_flat.content:
This policy will be:

*   Shared with students via assemblies, tutor time, and class time.
*   Available on Phidias for parents.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.9. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The policy's effectiveness will be monitored and formally reviewed every year. This annual check ensures the rules are working as intended and achieving their goals.
nodes_flat.content: The effectiveness of this policy will be reviewed annually.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.10. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: Our school strictly follows the official JCQ Guidelines for all exams. Parents can find full details on exam regulations, especially concerning malpractice, directly on the JCQ website: www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice. This ensures consistent and fair procedures for all student assessments.
nodes_flat.content: The school follows JCQ Guidelines on all exams. Full details of official regulations can be found at: [www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice](http://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice)

nodes_flat.title: 9.2. Internal Appeals Procedure & Assessment Reviews (Including AI Misuse Awareness)
nodes_flat.summary: Appealing grades for coursework/NEAs and requesting "Enquiries About Results" (EARs) for exam marks are detailed here. Parents can appeal internal assessment marks (if incorrect/unfair) to the Exams Officer within 5 school days; a Review Panel gives a final decision within 10 school days. Appeals are kept for 5 school years. For external exams (GCSEs/A-levels), EARs require written consent and submission within 2-3 weeks of results; crucially, marks can decrease. Students get annual guidance on academic integrity and "responsible AI use" (e.g., ChatGPT). AI tools are for planning/brainstorming *only if permitted* and *must be acknowledged* if used as a source. Misuse leads to referral to the Academic Misconduct Panel, potentially disqualifying work. Policy effectiveness is reviewed "annually." Parents receive the policy via Phidias. Reference documents include JCQ Guidelines and the Assessment Policy on how students are graded.
