step2.sections_text:
[Section 6: 6. Anti-Bullying Policy]
## 6.1. Scope

This policy applies to all members of the Imagine Montessori School community, including students, staff, parents, and visitors. It is relevant to all activities occurring within the school grounds, during school-sponsored events, and in any interactions involving members of the school community, including online or remote communications.

## Bullying Policy: Terminology and Principles

*   **Bullying**: Deliberate, repeated, and continuous hurtful behaviour aimed at causing distress to another individual. This includes physical, verbal, psychological, and cyber forms.
*   **Victim**: The individual subjected to bullying behaviour.
*   **Perpetrator**: The individual or group engaging in bullying behaviour.
*   **Cyber-bullying**: Bullying through digital means such as text messages, social media, or emails.
*   **PSHEE**: Personal, Social, Health, and Economic Education.

At Imagine Montessori School, we strongly believe that children have a right to study in a safe, supportive, and secure environment. Incidents of bullying threaten this and cause enormous stress to victims. We are therefore strongly committed to the avoidance of bullying in all its forms.

We believe that the principal means of preventing it is through the maintenance of conditions where bullying is less likely to flourish and is more easily detected. At Imagine Montessori School, all pupils will be known to us personally, and it will therefore be easier for staff to detect signs of possible distress. Moreover, we feel that it will be important that pupils have free and informal access to the Head and other staff. A formal anti-bullying policy will be of utmost importance.

As part of our Anti-Bullying Policy, Imagine Montessori School believes that all children and adults have the right to live in a supportive, caring environment in which pupils feel safe and free from bullying and harassment that may include cyber-bullying and prejudice-based bullying related to special educational needs, sexual orientation, sex, race, religion and belief, gender reassignment, or disability, and the use of discriminatory language. Bullying can occur through several types of anti-social behaviour.

## 6.4. Policy Objectives

*   To enable all members of the school community to understand what constitutes bullying, making it clear to children, staff, and families that bullying is completely unacceptable and that children and young people have the right to be protected from physical, written, and oral abuse or intimidation.
*   To prevent bullying by raising awareness with all members of the school community, ensuring they have a clear understanding of what bullying behaviour is.
*   To ensure that all members of the school community know and understand the school policy on bullying and follow the established procedures when bullying is suspected or reported.
*   To ensure that all pupils are confident to report bullying if they feel they are being bullied or they witness bullying towards another student.
*   To involve students actively in developing the anti-bullying ethos in the school.
*   To create and maintain a caring, kind, non-violent, and non-threatening atmosphere in the school and raise the school community awareness through educational opportunities that arise, such as PSHE and citizenship activities, circle time, and assemblies.
*   Students are entitled:
    *   To feel welcome and safe and not be made to feel unhappy by others.
    *   Not to be frightened by others.
    *   Not to be touched in an inappropriate manner.
    *   Not to be teased about birth, gender, age, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, opinion, mental capacity, religion and belief, education, economic status, personality, or any other personal or social circumstance or condition.
    *   Not to be made unhappy by unpleasant comments, notes, letters, text messages, e-mails, phone calls, any form of gossip, or by any other means.
    *   To feel confident to report any concerns.

## Rationale and Aims Regarding Bullying

All members of the school have an entitlement to be in an educational environment that is safe, positive, secure, and supportive. Bullying interferes with this.

Bullying can be defined as a deliberate act done to cause distress solely to give a feeling of power, status, or other gratification to the bully. Bullying can range from ostracising, name-calling, teasing, threats, threatening or aggressive language or body posturing, and extortion, through physical intimidation, assault on persons and/or their property. It can be an unresolved single frightening incident that casts a shadow over a child's life, or a series of such incidents.

Staff, parents, and children work together to create a happy, caring, and safe learning environment. Bullying, whether verbal, physical, or indirect, is not tolerated. It is everyone's responsibility to try to prevent occurrences of bullying and to deal with any incidents quickly and effectively.

The victim(s), their friend(s), their parent(s), or other interested people may bring bullying to the attention of any member of staff.

*   To provide a safe, caring environment for the whole school community, especially the children in our care.
*   To promote in everyone a positive attitude and high self-esteem.
*   To help individuals to understand and accept the consequences of their own actions.
*   To develop a programme whereby all incidents of bullying can be reported in a secure atmosphere and will be dealt with fairly and appropriately.
*   To help individuals celebrate and value their differences and to acknowledge and respect the differences of others.
*   To instil in all members of the school community that bullying is unacceptable and that reports of bullying will be taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon.

## 6.7. Implementation

**a. Framework for Preventing Bullying**

Prevention is better than cure, and Imagine Montessori tries to ensure that pupils and adults are educated in anti-bullying strategies. These include:

*   Ensuring that staff always listen to and act on complaints of bullying.
*   Encouraging pupils to tell a trusted adult if they feel threatened in any way.
*   Encouraging the caring and nurturing side of children through explicit teaching of PSHE/Citizenship combined with the Montessori moral awareness which has been developing within the school from when they started in the Infant Community (Pre-Nursery).
*   Working within the school through the curriculum to develop a caring, cooperative, responsible ethos.
*   Providing opportunities to discuss friendships and inter-personal skills, and using drama and role play to meet situations.
*   Ensuring adequate supervision in the playground.
*   Actively discouraging bullying.

## 6.8. Definition of Bullying

Bullying is behaviour which consists of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression from a student or students towards another student and/or these students place themselves in a position of superiority. This behaviour is repeated, continued over time, and deliberate.

Bullying can take many forms but have these elements in common:

*   Hurtful behaviour which is deliberate.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is repeated.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is continuous in time.
*   Hurtful behaviour which can take the form of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression.
*   Hurtful behaviour from which it is difficult to defend oneself; the bully places himself/herself in a position of superiority.

It can take the form of name-calling, threatened violence, actual violence, intimidation, isolation, ridicule, or other action such as spreading unpleasant stories and gossip, sending hurtful or intimidating text messages, e-mails, comments on social media, and telephone calls.

Communications technology (texting, e-mails, messaging, social network sites, Bluetooth) has also enabled a remote, potentially anonymous channel for bullying, popularly referred to as “Cyber-bullying”.

All bullying is designed to make the victim feel threatened, humiliated, and unsafe. Not all negative interactions between students can be deemed as bullying, and the school works hard to ensure that students understand the difference between bullying and ‘falling out’ or arguing.

All members of the School Community are expected to treat all potential bullying cases with utmost confidentiality, ensuring that all children’s rights are protected.

## 6.9. Preventing Bullying

The school is committed to the ongoing development of an anti-bullying ethos throughout the school with students, staff, and parents.

Imagine Montessori School will use the following methods for helping children to prevent bullying. As and when appropriate, these may include:

*   The school rules through circle time, during Personal, Social, Health Economic (PSHEE) and citizenship activities.
*   Social stories and drawings about positive behaviour and role modelling.
*   Reading stories about positive behaviour expectations and bullying, having them read to a class.
*   Making up role-plays.
*   Providing emotional support to give each child the opportunity to express how they feel.

**Methods**:

*   We will watch for early signs of distress in children.
*   We will listen, believe, and act when a child(ren) communicates that they are being bullied.
*   We will intervene to stop the child who is bullying from harming the other child or children.
*   We will explain to the child doing the bullying why his or her behaviour is unacceptable.
*   We will give reassurance to the child or children who have been bullied.
*   We will help the child who has done the bullying to recognise the impact of his or her actions.
*   We support the child who has done the bullying to learn alternate strategies to manage their behaviour.

**Good Practice for Staff**:

*   Be continually aware, watchful, and available.
*   Promote good behaviour and encourage the care of others.
*   Ensure children are appropriately supervised.
*   Report all cases of bullying to the Head.
*   Parents’ involvement and cooperation can be sought, and parents of children involved will be kept informed.

## 6.10. Identifying and Reporting Bullying

Students and families should report any suspicion of bullying immediately to any of the designated people below:

*   Tutor
*   Key Stage Manager
*   Head teacher

Much of the behaviour which leads to both bullying and intimidation is difficult to observe or can be seen as innocent when it is not. It is therefore essential that staff listen carefully to what children say and watch for any signs that a child may be being intimidated. The staff must always pass on their concern to the other members of staff working with the child and report these concerns to the Head Teacher.

**If you witness any unacceptable behaviour, how you should act:**

1.  **If you feel you are being bullied.**
    Tell someone.
    If you feel you are being bullied, you can tell a member of staff and/or your parent/carer (anyone you feel safe to talk to). It is not weak to do this.
    If you cannot tell anyone at school, you can tell your parents.
    Friends of those who feel bullied should let staff know of bullying incidents.
    No one deserves to be bullied. You have a right for this not to happen to you.

2.  **If you are a pupil and witness bullying behaviour.**
    Support the child who you observed being bullied by offering your friendship and making it clear that, in your opinion, what is happening to them is unkind.
    Encourage them to speak out on their own behalf by confronting the child who was exhibiting the bullying behaviour, or with their permission, confront that child yourself. Accompany the child who you observed being bullied to a trusted adult or suggest that you speak to an adult on their behalf.

3.  **If you are a member of Staff.**
    Reassure and support the pupils involved.
    Advise them that you are required to pass details on to their Guide and/or to the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour (Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher).
    The named person will keep a log of all complaints or incidences of bullying and record the way in which they were dealt with.

4.  **If you are a Parent.**
    Bring the issue to the attention of any member of staff, or to the Head of School, or the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour.

## 6.11. Taking Action

### Interviewing the child who has been bullied

**Interview the child who has been bullied.**

The child who has experienced the bullying will be interviewed by their Guide, who may ask them to write an immediate account of events. The Guide may also invite the child to discuss their own reaction and behaviour towards the child who bullied them. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them. The Guide may give them further support and advice if deemed appropriate.

### Interviewing the child responsible for the behaviour

**Interview the child responsible for that behaviour.**

Once the Guide is clear that a bullying incident has occurred, the child who was responsible for the bullying will be interviewed, as will any others related to the incident. Each will be asked to write an immediate account of events. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them.

### Actions and Consequences: Formal School Warning

**Actions and Consequences**

The following sanctions may be applied in accordance with the

### Actions and Consequences: Suspension

school’s Behaviour Policy:

1.  **Formal School Warning - Incident Report - Families Informed.**
    A formal school warning is a written or verbal notice given to a student who has been involved in bullying. It serves as an official acknowledgment of the behaviour and a warning that further actions will be taken if the behaviour continues.

    *   **Process**:
        *   **Investigation**: Before issuing a formal warning, the adult who witnesses the incident, the Guide, and the Head Teacher will investigate the bullying incident to gather facts, speak with witnesses, and understand the context.
        *   **Families Informed**: Clear and timely communication with the families of both the bully and the victim, ensuring they are aware of the situation, the actions taken by the school, and the support available. The school contacts the families as soon as possible after the incident has been reported and investigated. This is typically done via phone call, followed by a written notice.
        *   The school may arrange meetings with the families to discuss the incident, share the findings of the investigation, and explain the actions taken by the school. For the family of the bully, this may include discussing the formal warning and any further consequences. For the family of the victim, the focus will be on the support and protection measures in place for their child.
        *   The school maintains ongoing communication with the families to keep them informed of any developments. This includes updates on how the situation is being monitored and any further incidents or resolutions.
        *   **Warning Issued**: The school issues the formal warning, which may include a written notice that outlines the incident, suspension, or exclusion following the bullying policy. The warning is documented in the student's record.
        *   **Expectations and Consequences**: The student is informed of the expectations for their future behaviour and the consequences they will face if they do not adhere to these expectations. This might include suspension or exclusion if the behaviour continues.

2.  **Suspension.**
    *   **Definition**: Suspension is a temporary removal of a student from their regular school environment due to misbehaviour or violation of school policies.

### Actions and Consequences: Exclusion

*   **Duration**: Suspensions can vary from a day to a few days or weeks, depending on the severity of the behaviour. The student is expected to return to school after the suspension period ends.
    *   **Purpose**: The main goal of suspension is to provide a consequence for the student's actions while also giving them time to reflect on their behaviour. It also serves as a warning to the student and others that certain behaviours are unacceptable.
    *   **Types**:
        *   In-School Suspension: The student remains at school but is isolated from their peers and regular activities.
        *   Out-of-School Suspension: The student is not allowed to attend school or participate in any school-related activities during the suspension period.

3.  **Exclusion.**
    *   **Definition**: Exclusion, also known as expulsion or permanent exclusion, is the permanent removal of a student from the school.

### Reporting and Policy Review

*   **Duration**: Exclusion is indefinite and means that the student is no longer allowed to attend the school from which they were excluded. The student would need to seek education elsewhere.
    *   **Purpose**: Exclusion is used as a last resort when a student's behaviour is so severe or repeated that the school believes they can no longer be a part of the school community. This might be due to actions that pose a significant risk to the safety or well-being of others or a repeated failure to adhere to school rules despite previous interventions.
    *   **Process**: Exclusion usually involves a formal process, including meetings with the student, parents or guardians, and school administrators. There may be an appeal process where the decision can be reviewed.

This may be reported to the local authority.

These are minimum sanctions. In very serious cases, it may be necessary to make a report to the Police or Social Services. However, it is the policy of the school to attempt to resolve such issues internally using our own disciplinary sanctions, unless the matter is of such gravity that a criminal prosecution is likely.

The staff will discuss this policy and its implications on their practice at least once every year. Where possible, parents will be invited to contribute to the school’s policy and procedures to prevent bullying. The records of bullying offences will be reviewed by the Management Team once a year at their ‘Dashboard Meeting’ at the end of each school year.

In parallel, we will tackle the issue within the year group/class.

Each incident will be discussed at the weekly staff meeting to inform the other members of staff about how it has been addressed and prevent further incidents.

## 6.12. Record of the Incident

Details of the incident will be recorded by the welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila). The Guide will discuss with the named person for behaviour support what the appropriate and most helpful course of action is.

This may include:

*   A formal bullying warning where it is made clear what the bullying incident was and what would constitute a continuation of the bullying, including discussion of the incident or process in a hurtful way.
*   Clear strategies on what work would help both children (or groups of children) to be kind and loving towards each other.

**Monitoring and Review**

The Anti-Bullying Policy will be reviewed annually by the school’s Leadership Team.

Staff members will discuss the policy during designated professional development sessions to ensure consistency and effectiveness in its application. Records of bullying incidents will be analyzed for patterns, trends, and effectiveness of interventions.

**Reference Documents**

*   Imagine Montessori School Behaviour Policy
*   Imagine Montessori School Child Protection Policy
*   Local Authority Guidelines on Anti-Bullying Practices
*   United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC)
*   National Anti-Bullying Alliance Resources
*   School’s PSHEE and Citizenship Curriculum Framework

---

step2.raw_response:
When a bullying incident is reported to the school, the following steps are taken:

1.  **Reporting the Incident**: Students, families, or friends of those being bullied should report any suspicion of bullying immediately to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head teacher [6.10]. If a staff member witnesses unacceptable behavior, they should reassure and support the pupils involved and pass details to their Guide and/or the Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher [6.10]. Parents can bring the issue to the attention of any staff member, the Head of School, or the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour [6.10].
2.  **Logging the Incident**: The named person responsible for supporting good behaviour will keep a log of all complaints or incidences of bullying and record how they were dealt with [6.10].
3.  **Interviewing the Child Who Has Been Bullied**: The child who has experienced the bullying will be interviewed by their Guide. The Guide may ask them to write an immediate account of events, discuss their reaction and behavior, explain the process for dealing with the incident, and offer further support and advice [6.11, 6.11.s1].
4.  **Interviewing the Child Responsible for the Behavior**: Once the Guide is clear that a bullying incident has occurred, the child responsible for the bullying, as well as any others related to the incident, will be interviewed. Each will be asked to write an immediate account of events, and the process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them [6.11, 6.11.s2].
5.  **Determining Course of Action**: The Guide will discuss with the named person for behaviour support what the appropriate and most helpful course of action is [6.12].
6.  **Investigation (for Formal Warning)**: Before issuing a formal warning, the adult who witnesses the incident, the Guide, and the Head Teacher will investigate the bullying incident to gather facts, speak with witnesses, and understand the context [6.11.s4].
7.  **Informing Families**: Clear and timely communication with the families of both the bully and the victim is made, ensuring they are aware of the situation, actions taken, and support available. This typically involves a phone call followed by a written notice, and meetings may be arranged to discuss the incident and findings [6.11.s4].
8.  **Applying Actions and Consequences**: Sanctions may be applied in accordance with the school’s Behaviour Policy [6.11.s3, 6.11.s4].
    *   **Formal School Warning**: A written or verbal notice may be issued, documented in the student's record, outlining the incident and expectations for future behaviour, and the consequences if the behaviour continues [6.11.s4, 6.12].
    *   **Suspension**: Temporary removal from the school environment for a period of time, either in-school or out-of-school, depending on the severity [6.11.s4, 6.11.s5].
    *   **Exclusion**: Permanent removal from the school, used as a last resort for severe or repeated misbehaviour [6.11.s5, 6.11.s6].
    *   In very serious cases, a report may be made to the Police or Social Services [6.11.s6].
9.  **Recording Incident Details**: Details of the incident will be recorded by the welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila) [6.12].
10. **Weekly Staff Discussion**: Each incident will be discussed at the weekly staff meeting to inform other members of staff about how it has been addressed and to prevent further incidents [6.11.s6].
11. **Annual Review**: Records of bullying offences will be reviewed by the Management Team once a year at their ‘Dashboard Meeting’ [6.11.s6]. The Anti-Bullying Policy will also be reviewed annually by the school’s Leadership Team, and records of bullying incidents analyzed for patterns, trends, and effectiveness of interventions [6.12].

queries.question: Can teachers physically restrain students, and under what conditions is this allowed?

step1.outline:
[1] 1. Our Commitment: Imagine Montessori's commitment centers on Montessori philosophy: grace, courtesy, autonomy, curiosity. We value all individuals, cultures, and promote fairness. PSE (Personal, Social, Emotional) in Early/Primary and PSHE (Personal, Social, Health and Economic) in Secondary assemblies/lessons teach respect, tackle stereotypes, and empower children against discrimination. Our Equal Opportunities policy adheres to the Equality Act 2010 (revised June 2015), prohibiting discrimination based on gender, age, race, religion/belief, ethnicity, disability, and other factors. All activities are inclusive; staff uphold anti-discriminatory practice. Report unfair treatment to the school director. Objectives include meeting diverse needs, eliminating racism/sexism, promoting individual/cultural diversity, and preparing children for a multicultural, multi-faith society. We monitor progress annually to prevent prejudice-based bullying and build a cohesive community.
  [1.1] 1.1. Equal Opportunities: Our school is committed to equal opportunities for all children and families, following the Equality Act 2010 (revised June 2015). We ensure fair treatment and prohibit discrimination based on gender, age, race, religion/belief, ethnicity, disability, and other factors. All school activities are inclusive. Staff job descriptions include upholding this anti-discriminatory practice. If you have concerns about unfair treatment, you must report them to the school director. This policy promotes gender equality, cultural diversity, and supports special needs, underpinning all other school rules.
  [1.2] 1.2. Objectives: The school aims to meet diverse needs and advance equality. We positively respond to special needs of ethnic minority pupils, those with disabilities, learning difficulties, or social disadvantages. We eliminate racism, sexism, and discriminatory practices, reducing prejudice and promoting individual and cultural diversity. Staff receive equality of opportunity. Our curriculum prepares children for a multicultural, multi-faith society. We monitor progress through excellent teaching, learning, and assessment to narrow achievement gaps and prevent all types of prejudice-based bullying. We build a cohesive school community. This policy is monitored annually.
[2] 2. Dress Code Policy: Imagine's Dress Code Policy prioritizes Inclusivity, Safety, Practicality, Modesty, and Clarity, using gender-neutral "dress key principles" to respect cultural diversity. [3] 3. Attendance Policy: Imagine Montessori values attendance & punctuality; parents have legal responsibility from age 6. Early Years' Morning Work Cycle starts 9:00 AM. An "authorised absence" requires school approval (e.g., illness, "Sick Absence," "Excused Absence"); parental support alone isn't enough. "Unauthorised absence" is without permission. Parents must notify the school on the first day of absence, or a "first-day contact process" begins. Term-time absence requests are *not automatically authorised*.

Expected attendance: CH 80%, Primary 90%, Secondary 95%. Transparent Classroom and Phidias record attendance.   [3.1] 3.1. Scope: Imagine Montessori School values attendance & punctuality. Parents have a legal responsibility for good attendance from age 6. Our admin team works with families to ensure this. The morning work cycle for Early Years (0-11 years old) begins at 9:00. Persistent lateness or absence creates learning gaps, impacting progress and age-related expectations, especially from Year 1. For 2-6 year olds, good attendance builds responsibility, routines, and self-confidence.
  [3.2] 3.2. Terminology: Key school terms: Attendance is being present; Punctuality means arriving on time. Absences are either Authorised (school-approved, e.g., illness) or Unauthorised (not approved, even if parent supports). Persistent Absence significantly impacts learning. For Early Years, the Morning Work Cycle begins at 9:00 AM. Transparent Classroom and Phidias are digital systems used to record attendance and lateness.
  [3.3] 3.3. Policy Statement and Principles: The policy promotes high attendance and punctuality for all pupils, including Nursery/Reception, to improve achievement. The school partners with families, monitors attendance, and has clear procedures for identifying, reporting, and reviewing poor attendance or persistent lateness, including follow-ups for non-attendance and support for struggling pupils. Staff, especially class teachers, are key.
  [3.4] 3.4. Definitions: An "authorised absence" requires a legitimate reason, parent notification, *and* school approval (e.g., illness, "Sick Absence," "Excused Absence"). Parents must notify the school on the first day of absence; otherwise, a "first-day contact process" is initiated. Registration is 8:45-9:00 AM. After 9:00 AM, pupils are "late," recorded in the "late book" and "digital register" (or "medical lateness"). Persistent lateness (IC/CH >12/term, Primary >6/term) triggers support. "Attendance concerns" are addressed by a coordinator.

    [3.4.s2] Procedures and Responsibilities: The school maintains strict registration processes and attendance data, clearly communicating attendance procedures and expectations to parents. Admin staff record attendance, manage the Absence/Late Book, update Transparent Classroom/Phidias for late/early, and contact parents for unexplained absences. [4] 4. Behaviour Policy: Imagine Montessori's Behaviour Policy applies to all, fostering a positive environment. It uses a **Positive Behaviour Approach** and **Natural Consequences**, guided by a **Behaviour Flowchart**. Staff are **role models**. Good choices are acknowledged, not extrinsically rewarded. Inappropriate behaviours are tracked via **Classroom Reflection** and **Incident Reports** (Orange/minor, Red/major). After 5 minor or 3 major incidents, the **Student Support Team (SST)** contacts families, leading to a '1 DAY AT HOME' consequence. Extreme aggression can trigger immediate '1 DAY AT HOME'. Conflict resolution uses the **Peace Table**. A 3-tier system addresses behaviour: Tier 1 (teacher reminders), Tier 2 (Flow Chart, parents after 3-5 incidents), Tier 3 (**Individual Support Plan (ISP)**, parent-provided 1-1 support). Physical restraint is only for safety. Parents are partners, informed via email/dismissal, and via **Referral Tracking Form** for serious incidents. The policy is reviewed annually.
  [4.1] 4.1. Scope: The Imagine Montessori School policy applies to all students, staff, and parents. It defines behaviour expectations, outlines strategies for a positive environment, and details approaches for addressing behavioural concerns. The policy supports children's personal, social, and emotional development, fostering community. It is applicable in all school settings: classrooms, playgrounds, and all onsite/offsite school activities/events.
  [4.2] 4.2. Terminology: Key terms for managing student behavior include the **Positive Behaviour Approach** (reinforcing good choices) and **Natural Consequences** (learning from outcomes). Staff follow a **Behaviour Flowchart** for consistent incident management. Children resolve conflicts at the **Peace Table**. The **Student Support Team (SST)**, comprising the Head Teacher and school psychologist, offers tailored support. **Classroom Reflection** forms track minor, major, or unacceptable behaviours.
  [4.3] Positive Behaviour and Role Modelling: Imagine Montessori uses a "Positive Behaviour approach" to foster community and collaboration, ensuring a safe, positive, and happy experience. Bullying is not tolerated, supported by a specific anti-bullying policy. Staff are expected to be respectful "role models," using positive language and behaviour. They provide emotional support, engage children purposefully, and work with children and parents to develop positive behaviour. Staff also work as a team to meet all children's academical, emotional, social, and developmental needs.
  [4.5] Responsibility and Principles of Behaviour: Imagine Montessori's Head Teachers oversee all behaviour, including social-emotional development, supported by a Student Support Team (Head Teacher, school psychologist). Our core principles are Montessori's grace and courtesy, respecting all cultures, and valuing individuality. All staff are positive role models, with new staff receiving induction. We expect everyone to treat others with respect, fostering our school ethos. Parents partner with staff on positive behavioural supports, receiving updates and informing us of changes. The Behaviour Flowchart guides our responses.
  [4.7] Behaviour and Expectations: Good behavior is acknowledged (e.g., "Thank you for walking nicely") to foster recognition, not extrinsic motivation. Explicit behavioral standards are set. Playground respect means: asking to share/take turns, respecting others' space, and being kind & inclusive. For minimum expectations, staff use gentle reminders like, "How do you think X felt?" or "How could we fix it?" Core values—Respect, Caring, Responsibility, Calm attitude—reinforce expectations.
  [4.9] 4.9. Dealing with Inappropriate Behaviours: The school prioritizes preventing inappropriate behaviours, teaching children responsibility and tools. Physical interventions are only used in extreme safety cases. A 'Behaviour flowchart' and 'Incident Report' track incidents, classified as 'Orange' (minor, e.g., inappropriate verbal language) or 'Red' (major, e.g., property damage/theft). For any incident, natural consequences are applied, and families are informed via email (cc'ing a coordinator like Sheila or Lindsay) or at dismissal. After 5 minor or 3 major incidents, the 'Student Support Team' contacts families for a '1 DAY AT HOME' consequence: the child is picked up and stays home the next day. This ensures school-home collaboration. For extreme behaviours (e.g., physical aggression), the '1 DAY AT HOME' step can be applied immediately with Head Teacher approval. Removing a child for safety is not a sanction.
  [4.10] 4.10. Natural Consequences: Imagine Montessori avoids extrinsic motivation (e.g., sticker charts, point systems). We acknowledge good choices, but consequences are "natural consequences" aligned with our "behaviour flowchart." This process helps children take responsibility for their actions. Parents often ask about our "natural consequences" approach or the "behaviour flowchart" in practice.
  [4.11] 4.11. Day to Day Strategies: We manage daily behaviour using positive language, modelling, and group time to discuss issues and teach strategies. We focus on reinstating relationships after conflicts. Our Behaviour Flow Chart outlines natural consequences for minor/major behaviours, and Classroom Reflection tracks major incidents. We have a 3-tier system: Tier 1 involves teacher reminders; Tier 2 uses the Flow Chart, with parents contacted after 3-5 incidents. Tier 3 addresses repetitive major issues with an Individual Support Plan (ISP) and potential one-to-one support. Our Student Support Team also assists. We prioritize prevention, aiming to keep children from escalating to Tiers 2/3.
  [4.12] 4.12. Special Intervention Support: Tier 3: For Tier 3 behavior support, my child receives one-to-one assistance. Imagine Montessori, a private school, requires parents to provide this 1-1 support. A meeting explains the need, intention, and strategies. The Student Support Team (SST), comprising the Head Teacher and psychologist, oversees the process, meeting regularly with the 1-1 support and teacher. I receive a weekly summary from the 1-1 support, monitored by the SST. An Individual Support Plan (ISP) outlines strategies for learning growth, with weekly 1-1 summaries tracking progress against it for positive behavior intervention.
  [4.13] Removal from Class and Immediate Action: If a child is removed from class due to danger or unresponsiveness to reminders, it's a consequence of their choices, not a sanction. They're offered a choice: modify behavior or take a break, returning when ready. Special intervention plans may use this for self-regulation. Staff can take immediate action for inappropriate behavior, always relevant to the child. This includes removing short-term privileges (e.g., park-time, social lunch) or natural consequences like working alone or in a restricted area. A "classroom reflection" might be used based on severity.
  [4.15] 4.15. Disagreements Between Pupils: To foster social skills and conflict resolution, adults monitor children's disagreements, offering mediation and support. Daily "circle time" allows pupils to reflect on actions, using examples like "Positive," "Challenge," "Improve on," or "Enjoyed." For direct conflict resolution, the "Peace Table" is used. A child invites a friend, explains their concern, and the other listens, then speaks. They use a 'turn to talk' object to ensure fair speaking. The goal is to fix problems, not accuse. Adults remain nearby to mediate if necessary.
  [4.16] 4.16. General Principles: Imagine staff are positive role models, using positive strategies and age-appropriate solutions for behaviour. They utilize the "peace table" and "circle time" to model positive language (e.g., "I felt sad when X..." not "You didn't play!"). Emotional regulation (deep breathing, walk-away) is taught. Staff prevent issues for children on Special Support Plans. We reinforce positive behaviour, support confidence, teach empathy, and ensure consequences are natural, not negative. We never send children to corridors, use physical force, compare, humiliate, or shout.
  [4.17] 4.17. Physical Restraint: Physical restraint (holding/picking up) is used ONLY to prevent injury to children/adults or property damage. We try to inform the child beforehand. Serious incidents are catalogued using the "classroom reflection" tracker, detailing what happened, who was involved, and actions taken in the 'note' section. These details are kept in the child's personal file. Parents are informed the same day via the "referral tracking form."
  [4.18] 4.18. Inappropriate Language: Imagine Montessori School is inclusive. Abusive behavior, including negative stereotypes or discrimination, is inappropriate. We immediately address unacceptability by explaining what was done, the harm caused, and why it's not appropriate. We avoid personal blame or creating new stereotypes like "bullies" or "racists."
  [4.19] Managing Aggressive and Hurtful Play: We manage aggressive play (e.g., superhero/weapon play) by setting ground rules, encouraging "Stop, I don’t like it," and teaching children to accept "no." We intervene if play becomes hurtful or excessively rough, offering alternatives. For impulsive hurtful words, we use teachable moments, foster empathy via role play, modelling, and circle time, teaching them to "fix the problem." If behaviour is problematic, we involve parents to identify causes: insecure attachment, carer skill gaps, exposure to aggression, developmental conditions, or suspected child abuse (reported to Social Services). We aim for a unified approach with the school psychologist.
  [4.21] 4.21. Monitoring and Review: The Behaviour Policy is reviewed annually by the Leadership Team and Student Support Team. Parent, student, and staff feedback ensures it remains effective. The review considers behavioural incident trends, family input, intervention effectiveness, and new legislation. Updates are communicated, with training for consistent implementation.
  [4.22] 4.22. [5] 5. Anti-Racism Policy: Imagine Montessori School's Anti-Racism Policy covers all community members (students, staff, parents, visitors) and all school activities, on/off-site. Racism is defined as discrimination based on color, ethnicity, culture, or origin, including verbal abuse. It differentiates Institutional Racism (covert, collective failure, per Stephen Lawrence Inquiry Report) from Personal Racism (direct, e.g., racist jokes, name-calling, physical assault). The school promotes equality and diversity, aiming to challenge discrimination and support all. All suspected racist incidents *must* be investigated and recorded using **Incident Report Forms**, following our **Behaviour Policy**. Disciplinary actions include Headteacher warnings, exclusion from co-curriculars, or temporary/permanent exclusion. Victims have the legal right to report to police. The Senior Leadership Team and Headteachers ensure staff promote racial equality. The policy is reviewed annually by the Leadership Team, with input from staff, students, and parents, referencing the **Equality Act 2010**.
  [5.1] 5.1. Scope: Imagine Montessori School's anti-racism policy applies to all community members: students, staff, parents, carers, and visitors. It covers all school activities, including lessons, assemblies, extracurriculars, and off-site trips. The policy addresses both direct and indirect racism, whether on school premises, during school-related activities, or in any interaction involving the school community.
  [5.2] Racism, Equality, and Diversity Policy and Aims: Imagine Montessori School defines racism as discrimination based on color, ethnicity, culture, or origin, including personal acts like verbal abuse. We champion equality (equal opportunities) and diversity (celebrating differences) within our multi-ethnic community. We proactively promote racial equality, good race relations, and eliminate discrimination. Racism is addressed across the curriculum (PSHE, Religious Studies, Assemblies, Circle time). Our aims are to challenge all forms of discrimination, prejudice, and stereotyping; support every student and staff member to achieve their full potential; value diverse traditions and cultures; prevent direct/indirect racial discrimination; and remove barriers to equality.
  [5.5] 5.5. Definition of Racism: Racism is bullying, manifesting as institutional or personal. Institutional Racism, defined by the Stephen Lawrence Inquiry Report, is an organization's collective failure to serve people due to colour, culture, or ethnic origin, through unwitting prejudice, ignorance, or stereotyping, disadvantaging minority ethnic people. It's covert. If a child's behaviour/attainment is affected by racist bullying and treated in isolation, it's institutional racism. Personal Racism is direct, including physical assault, derogatory name-calling, racist jokes, graffiti, wearing racist badges, verbal abuse, threats, incitement, or racist comments in lessons. Imagine Montessori School challenges such attitudes.
  [5.6] Accountability, Responsibility, and Action Regarding Racism: The Anti-Racism Policy mandates the Senior Leadership Team and Headteachers ensure staff promote racial equality, challenge racist/geographically biased attitudes, and report suspected racism to HTs. All reported racist incidents *must* be investigated and recorded using Incident Report Forms, following our Behaviour Policy. Disciplinary actions for students include Headteacher warnings, exclusion from co-curricular activities, or temporary/permanent exclusion for continued racist behaviour. Victims' satisfaction is crucial, and they have the legal right to report racial crimes to police. New staff are inducted, and the policy undergoes a formal review annually, with amendments circulated to staff and families.
  [5.8] 5.8. Monitoring and Review: The Anti-Racism Policy undergoes an annual review by the Leadership Team. This process ensures its effectiveness and compliance with current legislation and best practices. Crucially, input is actively sought from staff, students, and parents to guarantee the policy addresses community needs. All updates or changes are then promptly communicated to stakeholders.
  [5.9] 5.9. Reference Documents: Key reference documents include the Behaviour Policy, detailing school rules and student conduct. The Anti-Bullying Policy outlines how bullying is addressed. Incident Report Forms provide a process for logging concerns. Also referenced is relevant legislation, specifically the Equality Act 2010, ensuring fair treatment and legal protections for all.
[6] 6. Anti-Bullying Policy: Imagine Montessori's Anti-Bullying Policy applies to all (students, staff, parents, visitors) on/off-site, including online. Bullying is defined as repeated, deliberate, hurtful physical, verbal, psychological, or cyber aggression, distinct from 'falling out'. It covers prejudice-based bullying (SEN, sexual orientation, race, religion, disability, gender reassignment) and discriminatory language. The school ensures a safe, supportive environment, preventing bullying via PSHEE, personal pupil knowledge, supervision, and fostering a caring ethos. Report bullying immediately to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head Teacher. Staff (e.g., Lindsay &/or Sheila for records) and parents can report to any staff/Head. Incidents trigger a 'Formal School Warning', 'Incident Report', and 'Families Informed'. Consequences include 'Suspension' (In-School (ISS) or Out-of-School (OSS), 1 day to weeks) or 'Exclusion' (permanent removal/expulsion), a last resort with appeal. Police/Social Services are involved if criminal. Bullying records are reviewed annually at a 'Dashboard Meeting' by Management Team and weekly at staff meetings. Confidentiality is paramount. "No one deserves to be bullied."
  [6.1] 6.1. Scope: This policy applies to everyone in the Imagine Montessori School community: students, staff, parents, and visitors. It covers all activities on school grounds, at school-sponsored events, and any online or remote communications involving our community members.
  [6.2] Bullying Policy: Terminology and Principles: Imagine Montessori defines bullying as deliberate, repeated, hurtful behaviour (physical, verbal, psychological, cyber). A "Victim" is targeted, a "Perpetrator" engages. "Cyber-bullying" is digital. The school ensures a safe, supportive environment, committed to avoiding all bullying. Our Anti-Bullying Policy prevents it by knowing pupils personally, enabling staff to detect distress, and offering free access to the Head/staff. It covers prejudice-based bullying (SEN, sexual orientation, race, religion, disability, gender reassignment) and discriminatory language. PSHEE is part of our education.
  [6.4] 6.4. Policy Objectives: Bullying is defined as unacceptable physical, written, or oral abuse/intimidation. The school's policy ensures all members (children, staff, families) understand this, preventing it through awareness and clear reporting procedures. Students are encouraged to report any concerns. The school fosters a caring, non-violent atmosphere, educating via PSHE and assemblies. Students are entitled to feel safe, not be frightened, not be touched inappropriately, and not be teased based on birth, gender, age, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, mental capacity, religion, economic status, or other personal circumstances. This includes protection from cyberbullying (texts, emails, gossip) and any unpleasant comments.
  [6.5] Rationale and Aims Regarding Bullying: Our school guarantees a safe, positive environment; bullying is not tolerated. Bullying, whether verbal, physical, or indirect, is a deliberate act causing distress for power, including ostracising, name-calling, threats, or physical assault. All staff, parents, and children share responsibility for prevention. Incidents can be reported to any staff member. We aim to provide a safe, caring environment, promote self-esteem, ensure individuals understand consequences, and have a secure program where all bullying reports are taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon.
  [6.7] 6.7. Implementation: Imagine Montessori prioritizes bullying prevention. Staff always listen and act on complaints. Children are encouraged to tell a trusted adult if they feel threatened. The school fosters a caring, cooperative, responsible ethos through explicit PSHE/Citizenship teaching and Montessori moral awareness, starting from Infant Community (Pre-Nursery). Opportunities are provided to discuss friendships and inter-personal skills using drama and role play. Adequate playground supervision is ensured, and bullying is actively discouraged.
  [6.8] 6.8. Definition of Bullying: Bullying is defined as repeated, continuous, deliberate physical, verbal, or psychological aggression by a student(s) asserting superiority. This hurtful behavior, difficult to defend against, aims to make victims feel threatened, humiliated, and unsafe. Forms include name-calling, violence, intimidation, isolation, ridicule, spreading rumors, and "Cyber-bullying" via technology. It's distinct from 'falling out' or arguing. All bullying cases require utmost confidentiality to protect children's rights.
  [6.9] 6.9. Preventing Bullying: Imagine Montessori School fosters an anti-bullying ethos. Prevention involves: school rules (circle time, PSHEE), social stories, role-plays, and emotional support. The school's methods include watching for distress, listening/believing/acting on bullying reports, intervening to stop harm, explaining unacceptable behavior, reassuring bullied children, and helping bullies learn alternate strategies. Staff are watchful, promote good behavior, supervise, and report all cases to the Head. Parents are involved and kept informed.
  [6.10] 6.10. Identifying and Reporting Bullying: Students & families must immediately report bullying suspicions to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head Teacher. Staff must observe signs, listen to children, and report concerns to the Head Teacher.

If you feel bullied, tell staff/parents; friends should inform staff. "No one deserves to be bullied." Pupil witnesses should support the victim, encourage them to speak out, or accompany them to a trusted adult.

Staff must reassure pupils, advising details go to their Guide/Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher, who logs all complaints. Parents report issues to any staff, Head of School, or the named person for good behaviour.
  [6.11] 6.11. Taking Action: When bullying occurs, a Guide interviews both the bullied child (who provides an 'immediate account of events' and discusses their 'reaction and behaviour') and the child responsible, along with witnesses. All involved write individual accounts. Consequences include a 'Formal School Warning,' which triggers an 'Incident Report' and 'Families Informed' process. An adult witness, Guide, and Head Teacher investigate; both families are contacted ASAP (phone, then written notice) and offered meetings. The warning details the incident and potential 'Suspension' (temporary removal, 1 day to weeks, either In-School (ISS) or Out-of-School (OSS)) or 'Exclusion' (permanent removal/expulsion), a last resort with an appeal process. Exclusion may be reported to the local authority; Police/Social Services are involved only if criminal prosecution is likely. Bullying records are reviewed annually at a 'Dashboard Meeting' by the Management Team and discussed weekly at staff meetings. Parents can contribute to the anti-bullying policy.
    [6.11.s1] Interviewing the child who has been bullied: When your child has been bullied, their Guide will interview them. They may be asked for a written 'immediate account of events' and to discuss their 'reaction and behaviour' towards the 'child who bullied them'. The 'process for dealing with the incident' will be clearly explained. The Guide can also provide 'further support and advice' if appropriate.
    [6.11.s2] Interviewing the child responsible for the behaviour: Once a Guide confirms a bullying incident, the child identified as responsible for the behavior will be interviewed. Other children related to the incident, such as witnesses, will also be interviewed. Each child is required to write an immediate, individual account of events. The Guide will then clearly explain the specific process for addressing the bullying incident and the next steps to all involved.
    [6.11.s3] Actions and Consequences: Formal School Warning: The "Formal School Warning" section outlines disciplinary sanctions for student misconduct. This formal action, a key consequence, is applied according to specific school policy. Parents should understand the procedures governing student behavior, the types of sanctions listed, and the process for addressing actions that warrant a warning or other disciplinary measures.
    [6.11.s4] Actions and Consequences: Suspension: Our school's Behaviour Policy details actions for bullying. A "Formal School Warning" involves an "Incident Report" and "Families Informed" process. An adult witness, Guide, and Head Teacher investigate. Both bully and victim families are contacted ASAP (phone, then written notice) and offered meetings to discuss findings, actions, and support. The warning, detailing the incident and potential suspension or exclusion, is documented in the student's record. Students learn future expectations and consequences. "Suspension" means temporary removal from school for misbehaviour or policy violation.
    [6.11.s5] Actions and Consequences: Exclusion: Suspensions (1 day to weeks) serve as a consequence, reflection time, and warning. They can be In-School Suspension (ISS), isolating the student on campus, or Out-of-School Suspension (OSS), barring them from school. Exclusion, also called expulsion or permanent exclusion, is the permanent removal of a student from school.
    [6.11.s6] Reporting and Policy Review: Exclusion is indefinite, meaning a child cannot return to school. It's a last resort for severe/repeated behavior, risk to others, or rule failure, involving formal meetings and an appeal process. This may be reported to the local authority. Police/Social Services are involved only if criminal prosecution is likely; the school prefers internal resolution. Staff discuss this policy annually. Parents can contribute to anti-bullying policy. Bullying records are reviewed annually by the Management Team at their 'Dashboard Meeting'. Incidents are discussed weekly at staff meetings and tackled within the year group/class.
  [6.12] 6.12. Record of the Incident: Welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila) record incident details. The Guide consults the named behaviour support person to determine the best course of action. This may involve a formal bullying warning, clearly defining the incident and what constitutes continued bullying (e.g., hurtful discussion), or strategies to foster kindness between children. The Anti-Bullying Policy is reviewed annually by the Leadership Team. Staff discuss it during professional development. Bullying records are analyzed for patterns, trends, and intervention effectiveness. Key reference documents include the school's Behaviour & Child Protection Policies, Local Authority Guidelines, UNCRC, National Anti-Bullying Alliance resources, and the PSHEE/Citizenship Curriculum.
[7] 7. Drug and Alcohol Policy: The school's Drug and Alcohol Policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and contractors on school premises, off-site events, and travel. It covers legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed/OTC drugs, illegal substances (e.g., cannabis), and "Legal Highs" (NPS). "Substance Misuse" means negative health/behavior impact; "Intoxicated" means impaired functioning. IMS strictly prohibits pupils from possessing, consuming, buying, selling, or exchanging drugs/substances, including smoking/vaping. Prescription medicine requires Head of Primary/Secondary agreement. Spanish law (Ley 42/2010) bans smoking on school premises. Staff must not be under the influence. Drug education is integrated into PSHE (Y7-13), covering risks, laws, and healthy choices, with Policia Nacional involvement. For pupil incidents, medical aid is first, followed by Behaviour/Exclusion Policies (including searches by Headteachers with 2 staff). Police involvement is at the Headteacher's discretion. Parents will be informed of incidents unless it risks the child. Parents under influence on premises will be asked to leave; refusal leads to police involvement. Staff receive mandatory drug awareness training. The Secondary Headteacher oversees the policy, reviewed annually. Parents can inquire about "pastoral care," "student well-being," "safeguarding," or "incident protocols
  [7.1] Scope and Terminology: This Drug and Alcohol Policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and contractors, covering school premises, off-site events, and travel. It addresses legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed/over-the-counter drugs, and illegal substances (e.g., cannabis), plus "Legal Highs" (NPS). The policy aims for drug prevention, education, and incident response, fostering a whole-school approach to well-being. Teachers will be skilled, and pupils receive accurate information and support. "Substance Misuse" means negative health/behavior impact; "Intoxicated" means impaired functioning.
  [7.3] Statutory Duties and Application of Policy: Schools have a statutory duty to promote pupil wellbeing and prevent drug misuse (pastoral care). The DfE advises a Drug Policy for incidents. Spanish law (Ley 28/2005, 42/2010) bans smoking in/around schools. The EYFS (2021, para 3.57) prohibits smoking, vaping, or e-cigarettes on premises when children are present, including offsite trips. Staff must not be under the influence of alcohol or other substances. This policy applies to all Imagine pupils, staff, parents, and third parties on school property, educational visits, off-site events, and public transport to/from school. It also covers all staff, visitors, and contractors. "Adults" are employees/supervisors.
  [7.5] 7.5. The School’s Stance on Drugs, Health and the Needs of Pupils: IMS strictly prohibits pupils from possessing, consuming, buying, selling, or exchanging drugs/substances on school grounds, during school hours, or on school visits. This includes a no smoking/vaping/e-cigarette policy. Prescription medicine use is permitted only with Head of Primary or Secondary agreement. Spanish law (Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre) bans smoking on school premises and selling alcohol in schools.
  [7.6] 7.6. Policy Framework: Our school's drug education, integrated into the PSHE programme, aims to empower pupils to make healthy, informed choices. It's based on British, national, and local guidelines, tailored to age.

For Years 7-9, the curriculum covers legal and illegal substances (alcohol, volatile substances, tobacco, cannabis), related laws, managing peer influence, and personal/social risks, including benefits of not drinking/smoking (and avoiding second-hand smoke). They learn about safe medicine use, 'experimental' use risks, 'dependence,' 'addiction,' and accessing local health services.

Years 10-11 delve into long-term health, mental/emotional well-being consequences, and wider risks to career, relationships, and future lifestyle. They further understand 'habit,' 'dependence,' and 'addiction.'

Years 12-13 focus on managing alcohol/drug use for health, decision-making, and personal safety, evaluating impacts on road safety, workplace safety, legal safety, reputation, and career. Specific topics include drink spiking, looking out for friends, and using licensed taxis.

We invite visitors like Policia Nacional to support our teacher-led programm
  [7.7] 7.7. Staff Support and Training: The school provides mandatory drug awareness training for all new staff during induction. Staff involved in drug education receive this training on an ongoing basis to ensure up-to-date knowledge and practices.
  [7.8] Incident Management and Police Involvement: For pupil drug incidents (possession, use, selling), medical aid (7.3) is first. Behaviour/Exclusion Policies apply, including searches and sanctions. Police involvement is at the Headteacher's discretion, consulting the Director, who is informed of all incidents. No legal obligation to call police. Parents under drug/alcohol influence on school premises will be asked to leave; child withheld for safety if collecting. Refusal to leave results in police being called. Serious concerns may lead to a parent site ban. See Appendices A & B.
  [7.10] 7.10. The Needs of Pupils: The school consistently exercises its pastoral responsibilities, ensuring pupils always access appropriate support and guidance. Parents can inquire about specific "pastoral care" or "student well-being" provisions for their child.
  [7.11] 7.11. Information Sharing: If the school has concerns about a child’s safety, the school will share information with law enforcement agencies and safeguarding agencies. This process is outlined in the Safeguarding Policy and involves working in partnership with external agencies to ensure child protection.
  [7.12] 7.12. Involvement of Parent/Carer(s): Parents/carers will be informed by the school of any drug-related incidents involving their child. This notification is mandatory unless, in very rare circumstances, it would heighten the risk to the child.
  [7.13] 7.13. Staff Conduct and Drug Use: All staff must follow the Staff Code of Conduct. Breaching policy leads to disciplinary action under the IMS disciplinary policy and other HR procedures.
  [7.14] 7.14. The Role of the Headteacher: The Secondary Headteacher is responsible for this policy, ensuring staff training. IMS premises cannot be used for drug production/supply; suspected sales are reported to police. Staff cannot promise confidentiality if significant harm is a risk, following the Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy. Parental substance misuse is considered; the Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator liaises with agencies. Suspected controlled drugs require a second adult witness. Headteachers have statutory power to search pupils/possessions (without consent, reasonable grounds, 2 staff). The Leadership Team reviews policies annually or after incidents/legislative changes, communicating updates to parents. Key reference documents include the Child Protection Policy, EYFS (2021), Spanish laws (Ley 28/2005, Ley 42/2010), and DfE guidance. Parents can inquire about safeguarding or incident protocols.
    [7.15] 7.15. Specific Procedures: Regarding information, staff cannot promise confidentiality to your child, especially if there's a risk of significant harm, and must follow the Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy. Parental substance misuse isn't automatically significant harm but is considered; the Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator will liaise with agencies if needed. For suspected controlled drugs, a second adult witness must be present. Headteachers have statutory power to search pupils/possessions without consent for prohibited items (with reasonable grounds), requiring 2 staff members to be present.
    [7.16] 7.16. Monitoring and Review: Our 'Monitoring and Review' process keeps policies up-to-date. The 'Leadership Team' reviews them annually, or after 'significant incidents' or 'legislative changes'. All 'updates' are communicated to 'stakeholders' (including parents) to ensure the policy remains 'relevant and effective' for everyone.
    [7.17] 7.17. Reference Documents: Reference documents detail policies for child safety, behaviour, and staff conduct. These include the Child Protection Policy, Behaviour Policy, and Code of Conduct. The Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021) outlines legal requirements for children's learning and welfare. Spanish laws Ley 28/2005 (26 Dec) and Ley 42/2010 (30 Dec) are cited, likely concerning public health. Department for Education guidance on managing drug-related incidents is also included. Parents can inquire about safeguarding, EYFS standards, or incident protocols.
[8] 8. Weapons and Dangerous Items Policy: Imagine Montessori School (IMS) enforces a "Zero Tolerance" policy on weapons, applying to all students, staff, parents, and visitors on/off-site. A "weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (real, replica, toy), bladed objects (knives, scissors), explosives, laser pens, and tools (e.g., screwdrivers) if used offensively. A "Prohibited Item" is any object posing a school safety risk. Staff can search with consent; Headteachers/staff have statutory power to search for "prohibited items" without consent if they have "reasonable grounds." Consequences for incidents, judged individually, include "fixed-term exclusion," "permanent exclusion," or a "Safety Plan" for pupil support. IMS prioritizes safety, uses a PSHE curriculum to educate on dangers, and expects open communication with parents. The policy is reviewed annually.
  [8.1] 8.1. Scope: Imagine Montessori School's (IMS) weapons policy applies to all students, staff, parents, visitors, and individuals on the IMS site or at school events (on/off-site). It details preventative measures, actions, and procedures for addressing weapons presence/use, alongside safety and support protocols for the entire community.
  [8.2] 8.2. Terminology: "Weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (real, replica, toy), bladed objects (knives, scissors), explosives (fireworks), aerosol sprays, laser pens, martial arts equipment (nunchaku), and tools (screwdrivers) if used offensively. A "Prohibited Item" is any object posing a school safety risk. A "Safety Plan" is a structured intervention for a pupil after a weapon incident, ensuring safety and promoting positive behavior.
  [8.3] 8.3. Policy Statement and Principles: IMS prioritizes safety with a "Zero Tolerance" policy: weapons are strictly prohibited. Staff take immediate action (searches, confiscation, reporting to authorities) if a weapon is suspected or found. A strong safeguarding culture and PSHE curriculum educate students on dangers. Incidents are handled individually, considering intent and risk, with actions from exclusion to safety planning, always prioritizing overall safety. Open communication with parents is key.
  [8.4] 8.4. Purpose and Aim: Our school's policy aims to prevent weapons on site and details direct actions for incidents, always prioritizing student safety. A "weapon" is broadly defined, encompassing firearms (e.g., toy guns, air guns), bladed objects (e.g., knives, scissors), explosives (e.g., fireworks, aerosol sprays), martial arts items, and tools (e.g., screwdrivers, hammers) if used offensively. Preventative strategies involve ALL staff understanding this policy, fostering a strong safeguarding culture, and using our PSHE curriculum to educate pupils on weapon risks and legal implications. Parents must be aware of this stringent policy, support senior leaders' decisions, and remain vigilant regarding their children's behavior.
  [8.5] 8.5. Searching & Confiscation: School staff can search your child with consent for any item. Without consent, Headteachers/staff have statutory power to search for "prohibited items" if they have "reasonable grounds." They can seize "prohibited items" or anything "harmful/detrimental to school discipline." Consequences depend on risk/intent: "fixed-term exclusion" (possibly with a "disciplinary hearing"), "permanent exclusion," or a "safety plan" upon return. A "safety plan" supports children, especially after a "weapon" incident, involving parents to manage risks and ensure safety. Each case is judged individually.
  [8.6] 8.6. Monitoring and Review: The policy's effectiveness is reviewed annually or after any weapons incident. These reviews assess the policy's clarity, preventative measures, incident responses, and gather feedback from staff, students, and parents. Amendments will be made as needed to address emerging risks and improve safeguarding practices.
  [8.7] 8.7. Reference Documents: For full understanding, this policy must be read with the Child Protection and Safeguarding Document, IMS Behaviour Policy, and relevant legal frameworks like the Education Act 1996 and Children Act 1989. It also incorporates local authority safeguarding guidelines.
[9] 9. External Examinations Policies: External exam policies detail "exam malpractice" (cheating) for GCSE/A-Levels, covering unauthorised items (phones, smartwatches), plagiarism, collusion, and undeclared AI tools (ChatGPT). A 5-step process handles suspected cases, leading to serious consequences: lost marks, disqualification, UCAS reports, and notification to AQA/Cambridge. Students must follow JCQ rules and declare AI use.

Parents can appeal internal assessment marks to the Exams Officer within 5 school days; a Review Panel gives a final decision within 10. "Enquiries About Results" (EARs) for external exams require written consent and submission within 2-3 weeks of results, with the crucial note that marks can decrease. Responsible AI use (planning/brainstorming *only if permitted* and *acknowledged*) is taught; misuse leads to an Academic Misconduct Panel and potential disqualification. All policies are reviewed annually and accessible on Phidias.
  [9.1] 9.1. Malpractice Policy – GCSE and A-Level Exams: The Malpractice Policy defines "exam malpractice" (cheating) as compromising assessment integrity, e.g., using unauthorised items (phones, smartwatches), plagiarism, collusion, or undeclared AI tools (ChatGPT). Suspected cases follow a 5-step process: investigation, evidence, report to exam boards (AQA, Cambridge), interview, and sanctions. Consequences are serious: warnings, lost marks, disqualification, UCAS reports. Students must follow JCQ rules, avoid unauthorised items, and declare AI use. Parents can find the full policy on Phidias. It's reviewed annually, adhering to JCQ guidelines.
    [9.1.1] 9.1.1. Scope: Students are informed about 'exam malpractice' (cheating), its definition, and the specific consequences for violations. The policy guides them on maintaining academic integrity in all assessments and examinations, ensuring awareness of fair conduct rules and 'what happens if' standards are broken.
    [9.1.2] Malpractice in Assessments and Procedures for Dealing with Suspected Cases: Malpractice is any act compromising assessment integrity or giving unfair advantage. Student examples include unauthorised materials (phones, smartwatches), copying, plagiarism, collusion, and using AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) without explicit permission. Submitting AI-generated content (essays, code) or failing to declare AI use is malpractice. Suspected cases follow a 5-step process: Initial Investigation (Exams Officer/teacher), Evidence Collection, Formal Report to exam boards (e.g., AQA, Cambridge), Candidate Interview, and Outcome/Sanctions by school/board. Parents should understand these rules.
    [9.1.6] Malpractice: Consequences and Student Responsibilities: Malpractice consequences are serious: warnings, lost marks, disqualification from subjects or all exams, even UCAS reports. Exam boards make final decisions, not schools. To avoid this, students must follow JCQ rules, be on time with equipment, bring no unauthorized items, submit original coursework/NEAs, and follow teacher guidance on tech/AI, asking if unsure.
    [9.1.8] 9.1.8. Communication: This policy will be shared with students during assemblies, tutor time, and class time. Parents can find the full policy document on Phidias. This ensures students are informed directly and parents have easy access to review the policy details.
    [9.1.9] 9.1.9. Monitoring and Review: The policy's effectiveness will be monitored and formally reviewed every year. This annual check ensures the rules are working as intended and achieving their goals.
    [9.1.10] 9.1.10. Reference Documents: Our school strictly follows the official JCQ Guidelines for all exams. Parents can find full details on exam regulations, especially concerning malpractice, directly on the JCQ website: www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice. This ensures consistent and fair procedures for all student assessments.
  [9.2] 9.2. Internal Appeals Procedure & Assessment Reviews (Including AI Misuse Awareness): Appealing grades for coursework/NEAs and requesting "Enquiries About Results" (EARs) for exam marks are detailed here. Parents can appeal internal assessment marks (if incorrect/unfair) to the Exams Officer within 5 school days; a Review Panel gives a final decision within 10 school days. Appeals are kept for 5 school years. For external exams (GCSEs/A-levels), EARs require written consent and submission within 2-3 weeks of results; crucially, marks can decrease. Students get annual guidance on academic integrity and "responsible AI use" (e.g., ChatGPT). AI tools are for planning/brainstorming *only if permitted* and *must be acknowledged* if used as a source. Misuse leads to referral to the Academic Misconduct Panel, potentially disqualifying work. Policy effectiveness is reviewed "annually." Parents receive the policy via Phidias. Reference documents include JCQ Guidelines and the Assessment Policy on how students are graded.
    [9.2.1] 9.2.1. Scope: This document details procedures for appealing grades on coursework and non-exam assessments. It also covers how to request "Enquiries About Results" (EARs) for exam marks. Furthermore, it outlines how students are educated on "academic integrity," including the appropriate use of AI tools.
    [9.2.2] Internal Appeals Process for Internal Assessments and NEAs: Parents can appeal internal assessment or NEA (coursework/non-exam assessment) marks if deemed incorrect or unfair. The process begins with an informal discussion with the subject teacher. If unresolved, a formal written appeal must be submitted to the Exams Officer within 5 school days, detailing grounds like a marking error. A Review Panel, comprising staff not involved in the original assessment, will then review the work. A written outcome, which is the final internal decision, is provided within 10 school days. All appeals must conclude before final marks are submitted to the exam board. Parent appeals are also kept on file for 5 school years.
    [9.2.4] 9.2.4. Enquiries About Results (EARs) – External Assessments: Concerned about your child's external exam results (GCSEs, A-levels)? You can request an Enquiries About Results (EAR) review. Discuss with the Exams Officer or subject teacher first. Written consent is mandatory (student or parent if under 18). Submit requests within the exam board's deadline, usually 2-3 weeks after results day. A school fee may apply unless the result changes. EAR types include clerical checks, marking reviews, and coursework moderation reviews. Crucially, marks can decrease during a moderation review.
    [9.2.5] Academic Integrity and AI Usage: Students receive annual guidance on academic integrity, covering plagiarism, misconduct, and responsible AI use (e.g., ChatGPT, Grammarly). AI tools are for planning, brainstorming, or revision *only if permitted by the teacher*. They must *not* be used to complete assessable work unless explicitly authorized, and any use as a source *must be acknowledged*. Misuse leads to referral to the Academic Misconduct Panel or Head of Year, potentially disqualifying the assessment or disciplinary action. For external exams, exam boards may be notified of suspected malpractice.
    [9.2.7] 9.2.7. Communication of Procedures: Policy communication procedures are clear: students learn about this policy during assemblies, tutor time, or in class. Parents will receive the policy directly through Phidias.
    [9.2.8] 9.2.8. Monitoring and Review: The "Monitoring and Review" process ensures the policy's effectiveness is assessed. A key rule is the annual review, meaning the policy's performance and impact will be evaluated once every year. This systematic check helps determine if the policy is achieving its goals. A parent might inquire, "How frequently is this policy's effectiveness reviewed?" The concrete answer is "annually."
    [9.2.9] 9.2.9. Reference Documents: Reference documents cover JCQ Guidelines on Appeals and EARs (Enquiries About Results) for challenging exam grades. Parents can review Imagine Montessori School's Assessment Policy on how students are graded, and the AI Usage and Academic Integrity Guidance for ethical AI use. The Behaviour for Learning Policy outlines conduct expectations. Records of internal assessments and appeals are also maintained.
